articles.title: Charity 'heartbroken' as food ruined in break-in
articles.summary: FareShare North East says it lost 16 tonnes of food - the equivalent of 7,000 meals.
articles.content:
A charity distributing food to the most needy said it had been "devastated" after thieves smashed their way into its warehouse, leaving tonnes of supplies ruined.

They broke into the Teesside base of FareShare North East on Tuesday and cut the power to the charity's walk-in freezer and chiller units, meaning perishables like milk and meat had to be thrown out.

The charity said it lost 16 tonnes of food - the equivalent of 7,000 meals.

Middlesbrough hub manager Natasha Flanagan said it was "heartbreaking", adding: "We've got a really great team of volunteers and staff and we try our best every day to get food out to people that need it."

FareShare North East redistributes food donated by supermarkets from its warehouses in Newcastle and Middlesbrough to more than 200 community groups, including food banks.

The charity has been in Middlesbrough since 2023 and moved to its new base on Skippers Lane about four months ago.

She said: "Lockers were ransacked, the staff drawers in the main warehouse were ransacked, and then I've seen the chiller doors were left open and the freezer door which has caused significant damage to food.

Cleveland Police said it received a report of a break-in at the premises at around 07:40 BST on Tuesday.

It said an investigation was ongoing and asked anyone with information to get in touch.

FareShare North East chief executive officer Jake Hanmore said: "It stings when you come in and find that the warehouse has been broken into.

"The team that are based here day in and day out are absolutely devastated.

"We've not long moved into this warehouse and so to come in and find it had been broken into was absolutely heartbreaking for them."

articles.title: How LNZ Cherkasy become a wartime success story with few modern parallels
articles.summary: Ukrainian club were in fourth tier in 2021 but, despite war with Russia, will contest European qualifiers for first time It is time for set-piece training at LNZ Cherkasy’s smart, shiny training facility. “Arsenal!” one player says with a laugh as the grappling at a corner begins, the joke spreading among others clustered in the six-yard box. That is a level everyone here must aspire to but they are edging nearer. This week a club from what used to be one of Ukraine’s football backwaters will make its European debut in a wartime success story with few modern-day parallels. “We are making history,” the LNZ striker Mark Assinor says after the jostling has stopped and the session wound down. “But there’s been a change in mentality here and I think this is only a starting point for the club.” Continue reading...
articles.content:
Ukrainian club were in fourth tier in 2021 but, despite war with Russia, will contest European qualifiers for first time

I t is time for set-piece training at LNZ Cherkasy’s smart, shiny training facility. “Arsenal!” one player says with a laugh as the grappling at a corner begins, the joke spreading among others clustered in the six-yard box. That is a level everyone here must aspire to but they are edging nearer. This week a club from what used to be one of Ukraine’s football backwaters will make its European debut in a wartime success story with few modern-day parallels.

“We are making history,” the LNZ striker Mark Assinor says after the jostling has stopped and the session wound down. “But there’s been a change in mentality here and I think this is only a starting point for the club.”

On Thursday they will face the Belgian side Gent, old hands on this stage, in the second qualifying round of the Conference League . LNZ earned that right last season by finishing runners-up in Ukraine’s top flight: a phenomenal achievement given they were playing fourth-tier football as recently as 2021 and only turned professional the previous year.

One of the first waymarkers after a seemingly endless six-mile drive across the Dnipro river is a billboard reading: “Cherkasy, football centre of Ukraine” in LNZ’s striking purple tone. The country’s geographical heart sits a stone’s throw from here but the local club is now pulsing with ambition and belief. LNZ were top of the league at the halfway point in 2025-26, ultimately finishing behind the might of Shakhtar Donetsk, but something remarkable has stirred nonetheless.

“No one expected anything from us,” says Vasyl Kayuk. He is sitting in a restaurant opposite the club’s city-centre headquarters, helping polish off a post-training buffet laid on for the team. At 26, Kayuk is the youngest chief executive in Ukrainian top-flight history. “The biggest motivation and advantage is that you are young and nobody believes in you.” Along with a similarly fresh, energetic staff he is determined for LNZ to break a mould.

“The city never had anything similar to this,” Kayuk says. LNZ have existed for 20 years but, in a football scene severely hampered since Russia’s full-scale invasion in February 2022, are a rare example of a club growing at pace. Their owners, a locally based agribusiness from which the club takes its name, have sown the seeds with purposeful investment despite the turmoil that continues nationally and the aim is to build a long-term success story that acts as a beacon for Ukraine.

“We always say that we are not sprinters, we are marathon runners,” he says. “We want to build our club and become stable.” LNZ exceeded every projection in only their third top-flight season, having finished 12th the year before. When they caused shockwaves with a 4-1 win at Shakhtar last October, Assinor among the scorers, an improbable title charge was on. A run of draws later in the campaign meant Shakhtar pulled away but foundations are being laid to ensure LNZ remain serious contenders.

A rare older face in such a youthful setup, their manager Vitaliy Ponomaryov applies the words of caution. “I don’t think that, if we celebrate this success today, it will be of use to us tomorrow,” he says, back at the training ground. “I literally told the players today that we have to forget everything, set new goals, and continue to grow.”

In some ways the 52-year-old Ponomaryov is from the Ukrainian old school: a straight-talking and no-frills coach not given to effusiveness. But he has hidden depths, including a spell officiating in top-flight games as an assistant referee during the 2000s. Since moving last year from Rukh Lviv, where he worked with Kayuk, he has created a flexible, high-functioning side comfortable playing in a mixture of styles.

“It had been difficult the previous season but he has come in and given us a winning mentality,” Assinor says. The Ghanaian was a dazzling find in 2024 after stints with obscure outfits in Poland and Slovakia, perfectly embodying a scouting setup that is not geared towards paying huge fees. “When we realised we had a chance of achieving something, everyone started to believe. He motivated everybody when he came in.”

LNZ will host the first leg against Gent in the Polish city Plock. Ukrainian sides are still required to play their European games abroad because of the precarious situation at home. It touches every part of life even at a vibrant, forward-looking club like this. In the training ground gym one of the physios, Artur Pohorilyi, describes his time as a war medic and how he survived a blast near Izyum in 2023. A club doctor, Roman Mishchenko, spends time working at a hospital for injured soldiers in Cherkasy to which LNZ also donate money. Mishchenko also works in Ukraine’s sophisticated system for amputee footballers .

A few miles along the river, in a repurposed Soviet-era sanatorium, LNZ’s academy base nurtures the stars of tomorrow. The under-16s coach, Alexei Bulgakov, worked for the now-dormant FC Mariupol before Russia attacked and took the city. He managed to escape but the cost, his home destroyed and separation from his family, was unimaginable. Bulgakov speaks vividly about a life growing up steeped in local football, describing the games between 52 teams of workers at Mariupol’s huge Ilyich steel plant.

Among his current charges are a number of youngsters from illegally occupied parts of Ukraine. Some of their parents remain in those places. “We speak to each other, talk about sights and buildings in our cities, and expect that some day we will be able to return,” he says. “The club understands how hard things are for young players from these areas, and it’s very happy to give them opportunities.”

They hope for futures in a rapidly expanding LNZ setup. The training ground is new and impressive by Ukrainian standards but considered to be only 35% complete, with three more pitches, an indoor dome and new stadium planned for the site. That process may take five more years. Crowds at their ageing city-centre ground trebled last season to more than 2,000, which is no small feat in wartime. Children below 14 can attend for free and a matchgoing culture is being fostered around a city finally making its name in elite football.

“We want to make this city purple, our special colour,” Kayuk says. “This club is for our people but we also want Ukrainians to associate themselves with us, with the country’s centre. And that when they think of Cherkasy, they think of football. We want to become a real power in our league.” A tussle with Arsenal’s corner specialists still looks some way off, but Gent could be the first foreign opponents to feel the purple wave’s emerging force.

articles.title: Don’t like the result? Don’t worry, sport no longer ends on the field of play | Jonathan Liew
articles.summary: From the wrong headset in an F2 garage to Fifa overturning a red card, we are in a landscape where fairness is the enemy A funny and very small thing happened at the Formula Two race at Spa on Sunday. Noel León, of Campos Racing, carved his way through the field to finish seventh, earning himself six points in the drivers’ championship, and promptly left the circuit to go and do whatever it is Noel León likes to do after a race. Take a nap, hit the Belgian brews, congratulate himself on his palindromic name – whatever. Anyway, while he was doing that, more than three hours after the chequered flag, the FIA governing body called a meeting and decided to disqualify him. The reason: a serious breach of appendix 5 and article 21.5 of the F2 Sporting Regulations – and assuming you don’t have those to hand, it’s the rule governing the use of headsets. During the race, a guest of León had been spotted in the Campos garage wearing what was deemed the wrong type of headset. According to the aforementioned appendix 5, only listening devices may be used by non-team personnel. This headset had a microphone, albeit upturned and unused. Continue reading...
articles.content:
From the wrong headset in an F2 garage to Fifa overturning a red card, we are in a landscape where fairness is the enemy

A funny and very small thing happened at the Formula Two race at Spa on Sunday. Noel León, of Campos Racing, carved his way through the field to finish seventh, earning himself six points in the drivers’ championship, and promptly left the circuit to go and do whatever it is Noel León likes to do after a race. Take a nap, hit the Belgian brews, congratulate himself on his palindromic name – whatever. Anyway, while he was doing that, more than three hours after the chequered flag, the FIA governing body called a meeting and decided to disqualify him.

The reason: a serious breach of appendix 5 and article 21.5 of the F2 Sporting Regulations – and assuming you don’t have those to hand, it’s the rule governing the use of headsets. During the race, a guest of León had been spotted in the Campos garage wearing what was deemed the wrong type of headset. According to the aforementioned appendix 5, only listening devices may be used by non-team personnel. This headset had a microphone, albeit upturned and unused.

Never mind that the guest had at no point attempted to communicate with the driver. Never mind the fact that León had no knowledge of any offence, no means of detecting or preventing it, and derived no advantage whatsoever from the presence of an extra unused microphone in the pit lane. How would you even cheat in this scenario, anyway? What would you even say? “Hey! Drive faster!”

We could of course devote an entire column to the pedantic weirdness of the FIA rulebook, its unshakeable fondness for petty penalties and meaningless bureaucracy. Watch any F1 race and invariably within a few laps the screen will flash up with the ominous words “FIA Stewards”, followed by some piddling infraction demanding immediate, race‑moulding punishment. (“8.5-second time penalty for Car 34 – Eating A Twix At The Wheel”).

But for the most part, at least the FIA dispenses its justice in a hurry. And in any case, the point here is a larger one. To an increasing and alarming extent, sport no longer ends on the field of play. The result is no longer the result. What happens out there is often simply a placeholder, a basis for negotiation, litigation and re-litigation: not the end of an argument but merely the start of a new one.

And this is a process that takes many forms. Debate, discourse, postmortem, pub debrief: this has always been part of the essential fabric of sport, and you would scarcely wish it otherwise. Complaining about a sporting outcome is one thing. Challenging it is another thing entirely, and a phenomenon that goes well beyond Daddy Trump and his recent invocations at the World Cup and the Open golf .

In 1990, the recently established court of arbitration for sport in Lausanne received a grand total of seven requests. As recently as the 2000s, it would hear no more than a few dozen cases a year. Now that number is close to 1,000, ranging from the minor to the seismic, from whether the Polish winger Konrad Michalak is owed unpaid wages by his former Egyptian club Zamalek, to the definition of a woman, to – at some point this year, with any luck – deciding who won the 2025 Africa Cup of Nations .

As you probably remember, the game ended in chaos. Senegal walked off in protest at refereeing decisions before returning to the pitch and winning 1-0; the Confederation of African Football later decided that they had effectively forfeited the game and awarded the cup to Morocco instead. And, of course, once you start the process of redistributing trophies and medals two months after a final whistle, why on earth would you expect Senegal to end it there? Lawyer up, gentlemen: to the death!

Closer to home, the Premier League’s legal battle with Manchester City has now stretched into its fourth year, with no end in sight and thus no real clarity on exactly how seriously we should take all those league titles they won under three coaches. City, as ever, fiercely deny any wrongdoing. And yet whatever the findings of the tribunal, this is basically an insane thing to happen to a sporting competition: close to half a decade in which its very history and legacy, an entire era of English football, is being argued very slowly, in a room, in secret.

Even a cursory glance at Cas’s inbox for the next few months will reveal that football is obviously the main culprit here: too big and too rich, too toxic and too tribal, too loosely and incompetently governed. At virtually every level of the game, from non‑league all the way to the World Cup, football has long been administrated along the principle that you take the money first, and work out the details later. Perhaps this is why the Lausanne dockets are replete with agent disputes, transfer disputes, competition disputes, quibbles over financial regulations and who gets to levy them.

And so when Donald Trump successfully lobbied Fifa to overturn a red card awarded to a player he had almost certainly never heard of before June 2026, it was not some shocking rupture but an act entirely on trend, for football as well as Trump himself. Don’t like the result? There is an entire apparatus constructed to indulge you: lawyers desperate for your business, administrators willing to bend an ear, armies of post-truth clones desperate to do your bidding on social media.

Perhaps all this was inevitable in a world quietly supplanting the idea of rules with the idea of brute power. Rules – in theory at least – imply fairness, the concept that we are all equal and to be treated the same. But what if – on the most fundamental level – you do not believe that we are all equal? What if your very purpose in life is to ensure that some (ie you) are treated better than others? What happens in a landscape where fairness is not an ideal, but the enemy?

And no, nobody is equating appendix 5 and article 21.5 of the FIA’s F2 Sporting Regulations with the disintegration of the rules-based world order. You might do that. I’m not going to do that. All the same, there is a kind of continuum here, one that leads all the way from the trivial to the tectonic: a portal of pedantry, a spectrum of sophistry, a continuum of choosiness. Once you start messing with sport after it has finished, even in the tiniest of ways, you can be sure the big stuff is only just around the corner.

And no, nobody is equating appendix 5 and article 21.5 of the FIA’s F2 Sporting Regulations with the disintegration of the rules-based world order. You might do that. I’m not going to do that. All the same, there is a kind of continuum here, one that leads all the way from the trivial to the tectonic: a portal of pedantry, a spectrum of sophistry, a continuum of choosiness. Once you start messing with sport after it has finished, even in the tiniest of ways, you can be sure the big stuff is only just around the corner.

articles.title: You be the judge: should my girlfriend stop insisting we queue for hours at concerts?
articles.summary: Fay likes to get a good spot and watch the support acts, but Odysseus would rather rock up for the main band. Who is hitting a false note? You decide • Find out how to get a disagreement settled or become a juror Fay likes to be on the front row but I think it’s overrated Continue reading...
articles.content:
Fay likes to get a good spot and watch the support acts, but Odysseus would rather rock up for the main band. Who is hitting a false note? You decide

Fay and I love going to concerts – we probably go to five a year together. Fay likes to get there as early as possible, ideally before the venue opens so we can queue up. When the doors open, she wants to rush in and get the best spot, preferably in the front row.

I just want to arrive for the main act. If there are three or four support acts, I don’t think it’s necessary to watch all of them. If we have to get there before the main act, I’d like to arrive right before the main support. That way we can enjoy the concert and still feel fresh, not having waited for hours beforehand. If we do that, we usually still get a good spot.

I enjoy heavy metal concerts with smaller crowds, and no one gets there early as there’s no need. People push and mosh out. Usually, the mainstream bands, which I think are lower quality, have bigger audiences. We went to see Coldplay in Austria last year and we had to queue in the sun. Inside, we waited three hours for them to come on. By then I was exhausted.

Fay likes to be at the front, but I think it’s overrated. You can’t go and grab food or go to the toilet for fear of losing your spot. Plus, it’s hard to get in and out of the crowd.

At a gig at Wembley, we didn’t go to the toilet because Fay was by the barrier at the front. She deliberately doesn’t drink water so she can watch the show without having to go, but getting dehydrated is dangerous.

Fay is short and wants to get a good spot so she can see, but I’ve said she can go on my shoulders. She says getting to a concert late means we have to push through people to get a good position. We did this once and I managed to get us a space that was only 20 people from the front. Another time, we got in late and Fay still got to the front. Fay says she doesn’t like pushing past people and filling in the spaces of a crowd, but they are there to be filled.

I’m happy to get to a gig slightly early, so that Fay can see, but some people queue from the morning. I definitely don’t want to queue for hours before it’s even started.

What Odysseus calls low-quality bands are popular bands. He says good-quality bands are the ones that no one knows, which are easier to get into and don’t have crowds, but I don’t agree. Coldplay are my favourite, but he also enjoys them.

We have never been stuck in a crowd or unable to go to the toilet. We take turns to go to the toilet and get back to our spot easily enough.

Odysseus is 1.88 metres tall (6ft 2in), while I’m only 1.59 metres (5ft 2in). He can see everything regardless of where he is in the crowd. I’m the one affected by not being able to see. Odysseus will offer to put me on his shoulders but I wouldn’t be comfortable sitting there for more than a few minutes, so that doesn’t help me if the show is three hours long. We both love music and concerts, so who cares if we are a bit inconvenienced by adding three hours of queueing to our day? We get rewarded by being on the first or second row and can see the whole act.

Odysseus likes to arrive later and then try to squeeze past people, but it annoys them. He almost fought with a woman once to get me to the barrier. I appreciated it, but she’d probably queued for ages to get that spot. He will try to fix being late by pushing past people, but it makes me uncomfortable. He says: “It’s OK, it’s a concert, we can do that,” but it’s embarrassing. People need gaps to move freely and breathe.

We went to see Ne-yo and Akon recently and paid £500 for VIP tickets, but it was super-crowded and I couldn’t see anything. I’d rather just buy normal tickets and get there early. As for being dehydrated, I always set alarms to drink water as I forget, even if I’m not at a concert, so it’s no different from how I am in my everyday life.

I like everything about going to concerts. The waiting and warm-up acts are part of the experience, it’s not all about the main act. I want to keep arriving before the doors open. These events bring us so much joy what difference does a few more hours make if it means we secure a good spot?

At 6ft 2in, Odysseus doesn’t understand the pain of being too short to see properly at concerts. He should be more sympathetic to Fay and get there early. Sasha, 39

The case for the prosecution crumbles with Fay’s claim that “Odysseus almost fought with a woman once to get me to the barrier … she’d probably queued for ages to get that spot.” Dude, that is totally against the spirit and etiquette of attending a live music performance. Ali, 42

I sympathise with Odysseus, but if Fay can’t see and wants to watch the support acts, he needs to suck it up and be there for her. Who knows, he may learn to enjoy the full experience as Fay does. Sid, 63

Fay needs to be at the front so she can see, but if she goes with Odysseus, it means fellow shorties behind him can’t see! Does Fay have other friends who could be her gig buddy sometimes? Pushing to the front, as a tall person, is not going to make you popular! Donna, 34

With the price of concert tickets these days, they’re lucky they get to so many gigs. Maybe that’s why Fay is so keen to experience the full show? If Odysseus has no interest in the support, why doesn’t Fay head in earlier for the warm-up acts, then Odysseus can follow later for the main show and find her at the front. Clara, 33

articles.title: ‘A surprising success’: US, Mexico and Canada fans on hosting the World Cup
articles.summary: The joy, love, hope and fun provided by fans outweighed the commercialism, extortionate prices and ugly politics This summer has been so vibrant and full of life. I had my doubts but, as the World Cup approached and Boston got ready to host its first game, I started feeling the excitement and enthusiasm in the air. That mainly had to do with the huge numbers of Scottish fans who arrived for their match against Haiti. They were a delight, what with their shenanigans of putting cones everywhere, walking around in kilts and carrying bagpipes, jamming with the other buskers on the streets. They were such a fun bunch. I found myself getting hooked. Seeing people from so many different countries, kitted out in their jerseys, gave you the feeling that something was happening. Friends and colleagues who don’t follow the sport were as excited as me. I attended three games and my only complaint is the extremely expensive ticket prices. The fans who came only had wonderful things to say about American hospitality – but everyone agreed that the political situation in the country is terrible. It was heartening that the visiting fans were able to separate the administration from the people living here. Moon, Boston Continue reading...
articles.content:
The joy, love, hope and fun provided by fans outweighed the commercialism, extortionate prices and ugly politics

This summer has been so vibrant and full of life. I had my doubts but, as the World Cup approached and Boston got ready to host its first game, I started feeling the excitement and enthusiasm in the air. That mainly had to do with the huge numbers of Scottish fans who arrived for their match against Haiti. They were a delight, what with their shenanigans of putting cones everywhere, walking around in kilts and carrying bagpipes, jamming with the other buskers on the streets. They were such a fun bunch. I found myself getting hooked. Seeing people from so many different countries, kitted out in their jerseys, gave you the feeling that something was happening. Friends and colleagues who don’t follow the sport were as excited as me. I attended three games and my only complaint is the extremely expensive ticket prices. The fans who came only had wonderful things to say about American hospitality – but everyone agreed that the political situation in the country is terrible. It was heartening that the visiting fans were able to separate the administration from the people living here. Moon, Boston

The mood was a bit sombre before the World Cup, with store owners complaining Fifa had locked them out and people downbeat about the incredibly high ticket prices. But once it started the cognitive dissonance did not stop people from going to the many watch parties in town, which were filled with fun and joy. We were priced out of the real thing but the party still went on, preferably without Fifa. In spite of everything done by Fifa, we sent out the message that people, and not institutions, own football. As for highlights, the incredible celebrations after Colombia beat DR Congo – which extended for hours after the game, well into the night, going on for blocks and blocks – will stay with me. I don’t think the city had ever felt more Colombian. Yes, and Merlin the duck. Eme Guitron, Guadalajara

The first week set the tone: the notoriously fickle Vancouver weather was perfect, the pedestrianisation of Granville Street was a roaring success and it seemed as if everyone bought into the experience. I attended Egypt v New Zealand and it was a real bucket-list moment. It was a flawless experience from start to finish: public transport ran like a dream, the atmosphere at the fan parks and stadium was incredible, and it was a great game. I even managed to win a correct score bet! People really welcomed the world. They say “Canadians are born all over the world” and it was wonderful to see the various diasporas bring their colour and energy to watching the games on the big screens. We saw the best of Canada at the tournament: beautiful and welcoming. I have been banging the drum for Vancouver to friends and family for years. After seeing just how fantastic this city is, hopefully the rest of the world will travel here too. I will never forget watching my son open his first ever pack of Panini football stickers on the eve of the first game. Ben Lane, Vancouver

I lived in South Africa when it hosted the World Cup in 2010. Fans were based in Johannesburg or Cape Town, with the World Cup central to everything for five weeks. You could feel it throughout the country. But this time the host cities were way too spread out. I was in DC, New York, Chicago and Atlanta during this tournament – two host cities and two cities that could have hosted. Unless you purposefully put yourself into a football-watching location, nothing seemed to be different than a random Tuesday. Kyle, New York

It seems like people had a good time. Roads and transit were a bit of a mess and we are not used to seeing people lined up at bars and pubs at 9am – there was a shortage of beer. Vancouver hosted the Winter Olympics a while back and it had the same vibe. I personally am glad it is over. I want my boring city back, please. Eleanor, Vancouver

I attended several matches during the 1986 World Cup but it was impossible this year because of the high prices. The mood was amazing in the city, though, with people enjoying matches in restaurants, bars, public squares, fanfests and stadiums. The Mexican fans were wonderful and gave a good impression to the world. My partner is English and we have two sons who love football, so the best moment was enjoying the Mexico v England match together in Mexico City. Oliver Regueiro, Mexico City

We went to a fanfest in Philadelphia but did not attend any games because we ain’t ballin’ like that. Not even my eldest brother – who attended the World Cup in South Korea and Olympic football matches in China – was willing to pony up for those outrageously inflated ticket prices. Despite the shenanigans of Fifa and Donald Trump, the World Cup was a surprising success. We’ve loved having all the international fans as well as seeing our own varied populace rooting for ancestral lands. People still think our elected leader is a feckless monster – they aren’t wrong – but now they remember it can be a lovely place to visit. The camaraderie and exuberance shown by fans – especially from Norway, Scotland and Japan – reminds us all of the best of humanity. W Pfeister, Louisiana

The atmosphere in the buildup to the tournament was coloured with cynicism: about high ticket prices, Fifa’s disregard for fans, and a queasiness about Gianni Infantino’s eagerness to cosy up to the Trump administration. Co-hosting with them? We felt like we needed a shower. Of course, as is always the way, when the tournament started the cynicism had to compete with genuine enthusiasm for the fantastic football, the Cinderella stories, and the sheer thrill of hosting the world. I had talked a good game about boycotting as I loathe Fifa with the heat of a thousand suns, but my friends and family persuaded me I would regret not going to some games. So I caved and attended two matches, which both had a great vibe. The atmosphere in Vancouver was superb. The buzz going into the stadium was terrific and everything was extremely well organised. The host cities and fans did a hell of a job. Respect where it’s due. Allan Castle, Vancouver

On one level the World Cup was a success and on another level it was an embarrassment. A success because it brought the world’s game to a level of national attention that is refreshing – much like the Olympics. On another level, it put on display the dysfunction of the US government: referees denied visas, teams forced to train in Mexico , and ugly politics between our president and Fifa president. I hope the trilateral hosting is repeated in future minus the embarrassing egotistical displays. Wayne H Donner, New York

The mood was vibrant. We haven’t seen the city come alive quite like this in a number of years. Toronto did not get many matches but, given it is so multicultural, every game felt like a home game for the teams playing. Most people bought into the event. Also, having to host meant the city finally made progress around things such as transit and essential roadworks. Maybe we should host things more often. I was surprised at just how many fans came to our city, some of their marches to the stadium were genuinely impressive, especially for the Portugal v Croatia game. Canada is not just a hockey country; this World Cup has proven that definitively and I can’t wait to see how the tournament will change the game here. Prem, Toronto

Everyone has been positive about our foreign guests, and we are glad they had a good time. Our guests have behaved with exquisite politeness even while partying with great enthusiasm. A friend of mine went to a game in Atlanta and said it was the greatest experience of her life. Many people who generally do not pay attention to sports (including myself) have been glued to the TV watching the matches. I believe the tournament will leave a legacy of US cities being able to host these large events in a manner that delights our guests. Hopefully, the world will see us in a more positive light. The highlights were watching our foreign guests partying in our cities, enjoying our food, and generally enjoying the American experience. Also, watching the Scots march through the streets of Boston was very exciting. I cannot get enough of those bagpipes! They are what got me interested and made me start watching. Anne Landrum, Atlanta

I was lucky to go to five games and the fan excitement will stay with me for ever. Being able to see Canada play in my home town in the World Cup (and win) was something I never thought I’d be able to say. I want people who watched the games in Vancouver to have a favourable view of my city, and I think the way it was executed will do that. I want people to think of Canada as a country where everyone is welcome and celebrated. We are a country of immigrants and we are stronger because of it. Populism and xenophobia seem to be on the rise across the world, so seeing people from all over take such joy in watching their team was beautiful. There was never hate for the opposing team; there was never hate for another country or its people; and there was no bragging about how one person’s country is better than the rest. It was purely a party. Jayden, Vancouver

It was great. Our country has been divided (like the rest of the world) for political reasons for a long time. During the last six weeks we have been one again: one country, one team! We regained that sense of great hospitality we had lost due to nasty violence and headlines about drug gangs. I did not hear about a single incident around the games. I will not forget singing the national anthem during the opening ceremony with my family. Rogelio Torres, Mexico City

I was surprised at the level of interest. It always peaks when US teams make runs at World Cups, but nothing like this year. Everyone I know followed intently. I was fortunate enough to attend several matches, including US games in Santa Clara, LA and Seattle. It was an absolutely electric atmosphere in the stadiums; I’ve never seen anything close to it at US matches previously. Seattle was especially jaw-dropping given the noise, the whole stadium being on their feet for the entire match, and the weirdly appropriate use of Country Roads. With the stadium adjacent to the city centre, the atmosphere spilled on to the streets in a way the suburban stadiums can’t equal. Allan Timpe, California

I left my home state of Massachusetts to follow my team, Egypt, during the tournament. Some cities did a better job than others and it was noticeable how much the vibe was different when the stadium was downtown compared with when it was outside the city. In Dallas and Boston, you couldn’t really tell there was a World Cup; in Seattle, Vancouver and Atlanta, you couldn’t escape it. Overall, considering the barrage of negativity that rightly surrounded the tournament before it started, it has exceeded my expectations. I just wish more games were played in Canada and Mexico. Omar Aly, Boston

A success because it brought the world’s game to a level of national attention that is refreshing – much like the Olympics. On another level, it put on display the dysfunction of the US government: referees denied visas, teams forced to train in Mexico , and ugly politics between our president and Fifa president. I hope the trilateral hosting is repeated in future minus the embarrassing egotistical displays. Wayne H Donner, New York

The mood was vibrant. We haven’t seen the city come alive quite like this in a number of years. Toronto did not get many matches but, given it is so multicultural, every game felt like a home game for the teams playing. Most people bought into the event. Also, having to host meant the city finally made progress around things such as transit and essential roadworks. Maybe we should host things more often. I was surprised at just how many fans came to our city, some of their marches to the stadium were genuinely impressive, especially for the Portugal v Croatia game. Canada is not just a hockey country; this World Cup has proven that definitively and I can’t wait to see how the tournament will change the game here. Prem, Toronto

Everyone has been positive about our foreign guests, and we are glad they had a good time. Our guests have behaved with exquisite politeness even while partying with great enthusiasm. A friend of mine went to a game in Atlanta and said it was the greatest experience of her life. Many people who generally do not pay attention to sports (including myself) have been glued to the TV watching the matches. I believe the tournament will leave a legacy of US cities being able to host these large events in a manner that delights our guests. Hopefully, the world will see us in a more positive light. The highlights were watching our foreign guests partying in our cities, enjoying our food, and generally enjoying the American experience. Also, watching the Scots march through the streets of Boston was very exciting. I cannot get enough of those bagpipes! They are what got me interested and made me start watching. Anne Landrum, Atlanta

I was lucky to go to five games and the fan excitement will stay with me for ever. Being able to see Canada play in my home town in the World Cup (and win) was something I never thought I’d be able to say. I want people who watched the games in Vancouver to have a favourable view of my city, and I think the way it was executed will do that. I want people to think of Canada as a country where everyone is welcome and celebrated. We are a country of immigrants and we are stronger because of it. Populism and xenophobia seem to be on the rise across the world, so seeing people from all over take such joy in watching their team was beautiful. There was never hate for the opposing team; there was never hate for another country or its people; and there was no bragging about how one person’s country is better than the rest. It was purely a party. Jayden, Vancouver

It was great. Our country has been divided (like the rest of the world) for political reasons for a long time. During the last six weeks we have been one again: one country, one team! We regained that sense of great hospitality we had lost due to nasty violence and headlines about drug gangs. I did not hear about a single incident around the games. I will not forget singing the national anthem during the opening ceremony with my family. Rogelio Torres, Mexico City

I was surprised at the level of interest. It always peaks when US teams make runs at World Cups, but nothing like this year. Everyone I know followed intently. I was fortunate enough to attend several matches, including US games in Santa Clara, LA and Seattle. It was an absolutely electric atmosphere in the stadiums; I’ve never seen anything close to it at US matches previously. Seattle was especially jaw-dropping given the noise, the whole stadium being on their feet for the entire match, and the weirdly appropriate use of Country Roads. With the stadium adjacent to the city centre, the atmosphere spilled on to the streets in a way the suburban stadiums can’t equal. Allan Timpe, California

I left my home state of Massachusetts to follow my team, Egypt, during the tournament. Some cities did a better job than others and it was noticeable how much the vibe was different when the stadium was downtown compared with when it was outside the city. In Dallas and Boston, you couldn’t really tell there was a World Cup; in Seattle, Vancouver and Atlanta, you couldn’t escape it. Overall, considering the barrage of negativity that rightly surrounded the tournament before it started, it has exceeded my expectations. I just wish more games were played in Canada and Mexico. Omar Aly, Boston

articles.title: Cyclist who switched countries targeted after comparing Australia to ‘high school’ and England to ‘Hogwarts’
articles.summary: Matt Richardson left Australia to compete for England after winning three Olympic medals The track star says he has much better resources with his new national team Matt Richardson may have become public enemy No 1 in Australian cycling when he decamped to ride for the country of his birth – but the Commonwealth’s fastest man on two wheels reckons it’s all had “zero effect” on him. And if the flyer now in Team England colours thinks there’s any lingering animosity towards him, having won two titles in the green-and-gold at the last Commonwealth Games in Birmingham, he promises to just use it as “fuel” for more success. Continue reading...
articles.content:
Matt Richardson left Australia to compete for England after winning three Olympic medals

The track star says he has much better resources with his new national team

Matt Richardson may have become public enemy No 1 in Australian cycling when he decamped to ride for the country of his birth – but the Commonwealth’s fastest man on two wheels reckons it’s all had “zero effect” on him.

And if the flyer now in Team England colours thinks there’s any lingering animosity towards him, having won two titles in the green-and-gold at the last Commonwealth Games in Birmingham, he promises to just use it as “fuel” for more success.

The 27-year-old, who was born in the English town of Maidstone but moved to Perth at nine and went on to carve out a brilliant track career for Australia, caused a sensation by switching allegiance straight after winning two silvers and a bronze at the 2024 Paris Olympics. He received a life ban for competing for Australia, but despite early bitter criticism back in the country that honed his cycling talent, he’s since carved out a strikingly impressive career in GB and England skinsuits.

“I tried to block out all the negativity, and all the noise,” Richardson shrugs when recalling the initial furore surrounding his ‘defection’. “I experienced the way they decided to handle it at the time, and moved on as quickly as I could. Ultimately, it has zero effect on me. If, for some reason, the British public didn’t take it well, that wouldn’t have been very good – but it wasn’t the case.”

Richardson is still battling to be the top sprinter in the world, having won silver at last year’s world championships, behind the great Harrie Lavreysen and actually having achieved the unthinkable, defeating the flying Dutchman in the sprint at February’s European Championships.

He’s twice cracked the flying 200m world record, becoming the first cyclist under nine seconds, and has made it clear he believes his advances have been down to his recent cycling education in the UK. As he famously put it in an interview with the BBC – and a mite woundingly for his old paymasters: “A way I can probably describe it is the Australian program is like high school, and the British program is like Hogwarts.”

“We fly with more bikes, and more equipment, anything we need to perform at our absolute best at a given race,” he says. “I remember doing World Cups for Australia, and I would only fly over with my race bike. I fly to races now with about four or five bikes. These things really make a difference, when a final comes down to a-thousandth-of-a-second.”

Richardson’s main rival in Glasgow could be former Australian teammate Leigh Hoffman, who’s admitted his old mate’s presence in the England team spices things up for the track meet at the Sir Chris Hoy Velodrome. “The Aussies want me to smash him, the Brits want him to smash me and that’s the way it goes,” Hoffman said at the Australian team announcement in Brisbane. “Seeing him in GB colours probably does bring out a bit more fire in me.”

That’s all grist to Richardson’s mill. “I use it as fuel,” he said. “I always find motivation in something, whether it’s trying to hunt someone down, trying to be better than someone, or trying to stay better than other people.”

One advantage Hoffman could have is that Richardson will be in Japan racing, and must hot foot it to Glasgow so close to race day that he’s not even sure which of the four sprint disciplines he’ll yet compete in. “If I was to race in the team sprint, I’d fly in on the day and literally go straight to the velodrome and race,” he said, admitting it still might be possible to do the team sprint, sprint, keirin and kilo.

articles.title: River levels approach all-time low as drought announcement looms
articles.summary: An announcement is expected from NRW as some river levels are reported to be nearing all-time lows.
articles.content:
Natural Resources Wales said that north and south-east Wales were moving to "prolonged dry weather" status

River levels are approaching all-time lows across Wales as an imminent "environmental drought" announcement looms for parts of the country.

Wales has seen about 5% of the average rainfall for July, after a series of b ack-to-back heatwaves and a drier than usual April to June.

Afonydd Cymru, which represents Wales' rivers trusts, said low river flows were causing "significant problems", killing fish and other wildlife, while farming unions warned of failing crops and heat-stressed livestock.

An announcement is expected later on Thursday from Natural Resources Wales (NRW), who said they were closely monitoring the situation.

NRW announced last week that north and south-east Wales were moving to "prolonged dry weather" status - classed as a first step towards drought.

It triggered enhanced monitoring and checks of waterways and important sites for wildlife as well as advice for farmers and others who use water from rivers.

Huwel Manley, NRW head of operations for south west Wales, said a number of watercourses were drying up and "even larger rivers are running at lower flows than usual".

Rivers classed as exceptionally low include the Clwyd, Alwen, Alyn, Clywedog, Dyfi and Conwy in north Wales as well as the Wye, which runs through parts of mid Wales and south along the border with England.

Extra water was being released from reservoirs to support flows on the Clwyd and Wye, as well as the river Severn in the south east and the Dee in north-east Wales.

While there is some rain in the forecast for the weekend, it will take time for river systems to recover, Manley warned.

Parts of the country could see the declaration of an "environmental drought", he predicted.

This would mean that water shortages in rivers and the ground, as well as dry soils, were having an immediate and detrimental impact on ecosystems, rather than there being a threat to drinking water or household supplies.

But Manley urged the public to be careful with their use of water - for the sake of wildlife and the wider environment.

"Maybe have a shower rather than a bath," he said. "And then there are also little things that can be done, such as putting some water out in the garden for mammals and birds."

Huwel Manley of NRW urged people to be careful with their use of water

Gail Davies-Walsh, chief executive of Afonydd Cymru, said the country's waterways were already under strain due to pollution.

"The lack of rainfall and decline in river flows is really starting to exacerbate water quality," she said.

"We've already had a couple of rivers that have dried up and we're getting reports through of fish that have been killed because of a lack of water."

High river temperatures were also leading to concerns for the survival of aquatic life, she added, with water temperatures as high as 28C recorded in the Wye and Usk.

Davies-Walsh said she was worried that people's frustrations at water companies over sewage spills and leakages was making them less likely to heed advice about being careful with their use of water.

"I'm looking at social media comments and I totally understand those frustrations," she said.

"But we've really got to think about the fact that our water usage is directly impacting the flow in our rivers and directly impacting fish and other wildlife's survival.

Stephanie Hafferty says gardeners will need to adapt to the changing climate

In the horticulture and flower marquees of the Royal Welsh Agricultural Show in Llanelwedd, Powys, both farmers and growers were talking about the weather's impacts.

"People are going to have to adapt, think about gathering their own water," said Paul Thomas of the National Vegetable Society.

"In the spring when it was crazy rain, everything was delayed - sowing and planting -because it was just relentless, and then it was almost like an off-switch," added garden and food writer Stephanie Hafferty.

"One of the things I noticed this morning is the trees are going into autumn colour, and that's a big worry, it's not even August yet."

Elen Hughes of Trelawnyd, Flintshire, said the fields on her family's farm were "bone dry, nothing's growing".

"They've just last week had to feed the second silage cut to the cows because there's no food for the cattle."

Farming unions have warned of failing crops and heat-stressed livestock

"We've had really dry weather but also it's been really hot - and those things combined if you're a livestock farmer means excessive pressure on livestock," said Abi Reader, president of NFU Cymru.

"You have to make sure they have enough water and shade, and we are getting farms with springs and streams drying up," she told BBC Walescast .

"If you're an arable farmer the heat and dry weather have also forced a lot of the crops to come to ripening a lot earlier than they normally would.

"It's unprecedented early harvesting this year and farmers are going to see lower crop yields."

Welsh Water emphasised that NRW's drought declarations were different to its own announcements.

The company has already brought in a hospipe ban for 33,000 properties in west Wales.

"NRW's drought status reflects environmental conditions, including factors such as rainfall, river flows and groundwater levels," a spokesperson said.

"By contrast, Welsh Water's Temporary Use Ban is an operational measure based on local water demand and our ability to replenish service reservoirs with drinking water at the same rate, or faster, than customers are using water.

"It is driven by the capacity of the local treatment works and distribution network to keep up with sustained demand.

"This means different parts of Wales can experience different operational pressures, even if they are not in the same environmental drought category declared by NRW."

'We just need a bed' - Baby with 'days to live' can't access lifesaving treatment

Family of woman who lay dead in crash for days confronts mechanic who falsified MOT

articles.title: Butterfly city blooms in shadow of office blocks
articles.summary: A patch of land in Hull city centre has been allowed to grow wild to create a haven for insects.
articles.content:
Sean Clough says the discovery of the small skipper butterfly is "rare for a city"

A "butterfly city" has been created in the shadow of office blocks and a shopping centre.

Ten species of butterfly have been spotted in a "mini meadow" in Hull city centre, according to a wildlife group.

Grass and wild flowers have been allowed to grow on part of a site owned by the Land Registry, near St Stephen's Shopping Centre.

Sean Clough, from Hull Butterfly City, said small skipper butterflies had been breeding in the meadow, which he described as "rare for a city".

The meadow was established in 2023 as part of a scheme to encourage people to grow plants that provide food for caterpillars.

Clough said the small skipper needed grass left uncut throughout the year, "because the tiny caterpillar spends the winter inside a grass sheath".

He added: "Everyone understands the role that flowering plants play for pollinators, but equally important for butterflies is to have the right plants for their caterpillars to eat, because most species of butterflies are very specific about what their caterpillars eat."

The meadow has become a haven for other wildlife, including dragonflies and grasshoppers.

Sarah Duell, head of sustainability at the Land Registry, said the staff "really love it".

Initially, there were concerns that the land might "look a bit of a mess".

"But we got together and talked about the fact that it's going to be pieces of the grass rather than the whole grass," she said.

"Once it got going, we had a little look around and started to see some of the insects and some of the butterflies coming to the areas that we have left to go wild.

"It was really, really exciting for us and for the office in general."

Listen to highlights from Hull and East Yorkshire on BBC Sounds , watch the latest episode of Look North .

Download the BBC News app from the App Store , external for iPhone and iPad or Google Play , external for Android devices

articles.title: Rachel Roddy’s ratatouille and potato gratin recipe
articles.summary: The classic Provencal stew, but with potato for ballast and topped with a cheesy herb crumb We swapped the heatwave and melting pavements in Rome for a similar situation in London, which meant catching a train to Dorset felt something of a relief. Not that it wasn’t hot there too, but it was offset by the sea, a garden and a dark, cool house. The weather also meant that the always good July produce was superb and abundant: all the berries, cherries, peas, runner and french beans; but also potatoes, aubergines, courgettes, tomatoes and herbs – all of which go into this week’s recipe. My template for ratatouille is a mix of Jane Grigson’s recipe in her Vegetable Book and Robert Carrier’s version in Great Dishes of the World, plus the advice from a French friend to cook the aubergine separately, either by deep-frying (by far the tastiest option), baking, or air frying. The benefit of this step is not just flavour; it ensures the aubergine holds its shape better, meaning distinct chunks in the final stew. Continue reading...
articles.content:
The classic Provencal stew, but with potato for ballast and topped with a cheesy herb crumb

W e swapped the heatwave and melting pavements in Rome for a similar situation in London, which meant catching a train to Dorset felt something of a relief. Not that it wasn’t hot there too, but it was offset by the sea, a garden and a dark, cool house. The weather also meant that the always good July produce was superb and abundant: all the berries, cherries, peas, runner and french beans; but also potatoes, aubergines, courgettes, tomatoes and herbs – all of which go into this week’s recipe.

My template for ratatouille is a mix of Jane Grigson’s recipe in her Vegetable Book and Robert Carrier’s version in Great Dishes of the World, plus the advice from a French friend to cook the aubergine separately, either by deep-frying (by far the tastiest option), baking, or air frying. The benefit of this step is not just flavour; it ensures the aubergine holds its shape better, meaning distinct chunks in the final stew.

What I like about Grigson’s recipe is the initial lively simmer (with a lid on), which kickstarts the cooking, followed by an uncovered simmer, then a final livelier simmer so any wateriness disappears. You will have a good idea of how sweet or acidic the tomatoes are from tasting them raw, but taste again towards the end of cooking and, if necessary, add a pinch of sugar or jot of red-wine vinegar.

Ratatouille is a Provençal word, which, according to the digital dictionary Orthodidacte, is linked to two terms: tatouiller, which means “to handle repeatedly” or “grope,” as well as “to stir in water’; and ratouiller , which means ‘to stir in water,” “mix for a long time,” or “stir up the mud at the bottom of the water”. I am at a loss as to how “grope” can be applied here, but the rest of the terms can be applied in some way to the making of ratatouille, especially the stirring of the mud at the bottom of the water, which is a reminder to swirl the spoon deeply so the vegetables don’t clump and stick to the bottom of the pan.

Potatoes are delicious when simmered along with tomatoes until soft, but their addition does turn it into another dish, which is why I have called this ratatouille and potato gratin – the gratin being a topping of soft breadcrumbs, grated cheese, minced herbs and olive oil, which, under the grill, turns into a crusty top.

If you are eating straight away, let the dish sit for about 10 minutes, so the juices that have run free are reabsorbed, before serving with sausages, grilled cheese, slices of roast chicken or simply a green salad. While the top will lose some of its crusty edge, the dish is just as good and even more velvety at room temperature.

1 large aubergine, cut into 2cm chunks Olive oil Salt 1 large onion, peeled and sliced 1 garlic clove, peeled 2 courgettes, cut into chunks 1 red or yellow pepper, cut into chunks 1 all purpose large potato, peeled and cut into chunks 3 big tomatoes, peeled, or 1 x 400g tin tomatoes Sugar and red-wine vinegar (optional)

For the topping 100g of soft bread crumbs 2 tbsp grated parmesan 2 tbsp minced herbs (parsley, thyme, marjoram, say)

Either deep-fry the aubergine chunks until golden, or spread them on a baking tray, rub them with oil and salt and bake for 25 minutes, until soft and golden, or use an air fryer. Set aside.

In a heavy-based pan, warm four to six tablespoons of olive oil and the onion and garlic. After about five minutes, add the courgettes, peppers and a pinch of salt, and stir. Five minutes later, add the potato and tomato, cover and simmer for 20 minutes.

Check the amount of liquid and add a little water so that the vegetables are surrounded, then reduce to a simmer for 40-45 minutes, stirring every now and then.

Ten minutes before the end of cooking, raise the heat a little so wateriness bubbles away for a dense stew. Then, in the last minutes of cooking, stir in the aubergine and taste for salt, adding a jot of sugar or red-wine vinegar, if you wish. Leave to cool a little.

Tip the stew into a roughly 30cm x 20cm rectangular dish. Combine the topping ingredients with some olive oil, then spread the crumbs over the top of the ratatouille and put under the grill for a few minutes, until the top is crusty.

articles.title: ‘People are scared their neighbours will call the police on them’: how the hosepipe ban got caught up in the culture wars
articles.summary: While many people in the UK recognise the need to preserve water, others are furious and planning to flout the ban in their area. The target of their anger? Failing water companies and frighteningly thirsty datacentres ‘Plants are like your babies,” says Harry. And accordingly, he argues, most people would do anything to keep them alive, including break the law. This is what he plans to do this week – defying a hosepipe ban in place across large parts of England, to ensure his shrubs maintain a steady pulse. He may not be alone. On Tuesday, Thames Water became the latest company to ban hosepipes – a restriction that will affect more than 10 million people. Since mid-July, Welsh Water, Anglian Water, Affinity Water (serving Bedfordshire, Berkshire, Buckinghamshire, Hertfordshire and Surrey), Southern Water and Cambridge Water have all announced hosepipe bans. And earlier this week, South East Water extended its existing ban to cover an additional 1.5 million. Continue reading...
articles.content:
While many people in the UK recognise the need to preserve water, others are furious and planning to flout the ban in their area. The target of their anger? Failing water companies and frighteningly thirsty datacentres

‘P lants are like your babies,” says Harry. And accordingly, he argues, most people would do anything to keep them alive, including break the law. This is what he plans to do this week – defying a hosepipe ban in place across large parts of England, to ensure his shrubs maintain a steady pulse.

He may not be alone. On Tuesday, Thames Water became the latest company to ban hosepipes – a restriction that will affect more than 10 million people. Since mid-July, Welsh Water, Anglian Water, Affinity Water (serving Bedfordshire, Berkshire, Buckinghamshire, Hertfordshire and Surrey), Southern Water and Cambridge Water have all announced hosepipe bans. And earlier this week, South East Water extended its existing ban to cover an additional 1.5 million.

Harry, 24, has spoken to friends and family in his leafy corner of Essex who are equally fed up with the ban that has been in place on and off since the beginning of July. “Unless they turn the taps off, I’m still going to use it,” he says. “It’s another thing that makes people say they want to leave the UK, these silly little rules.”

Others are using the ban to stoke up division. Suella Braverman MP says it’s yet another “act of lunacy in Labour’s two-tier Britain”. The Reform UK politician was responding to a Telegraph front page declaring that benefit claimants are exempt from the hosepipe ban. The Conservative party chair, Kevin Hollinrake, also chimed in, calling it a “kick in the hosepipe”.

Regardless of this much more nuanced reality, the hosepipe ban has become the subject of a new culture war, an attempt to pit the haves (in this scenario, poor and disabled people, who are allowed more water) against the have-nots (able-bodied people with an earned income).

Why has the ban been put in place, anyway? The answer, as with so many things, is the climate crisis. In 2025, the UK recorded its hottest year ever . According to the Met Office, 2026 is already showing hotter and drier conditions than last year – with three heatwaves to date. Daniel Johns, the managing director at the non-profit Water Resources East, says river levels have reached “all-time lows”. In May, a House of Lords report said England faces a 5bn-litre-a-day water shortfall by 2055. Water companies, says Johns, have been left with “no choice” but to impose hosepipe bans because the “hot weather has led households to use more water than ever before, depleting rivers and reservoirs at concerning rates” – which, if left unchecked, could lead to even more severe water shortages.

But Harry says his ire isn’t directed towards vulnerable people who can still use their hosepipes. Instead, he blames water companies – which, according to water regulator Ofwat, lose almost 3bn litres of drinking water a day in England and Wales through leaking pipes. That’s five times more than even a nationwide hosepipe ban could save, says Greenpeace UK .

Another thing guzzling our water supplies is the rise of AI, which Harry also hit out against. There are 450-plus AI datacentres in the UK, with more planned, which require enormous amounts of water to cool servers. On Tuesday, the water industry warned that these plans were “fatally flawed” as the policies make “not a single mention of water”. Water UK said that more than two-thirds of existing datacentres are in the south and south-east of England, where many of the hosepipe bans are in place. The Government Digital Sustainability Alliance, a public-private group led by the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs, has already warned that AI is predicted to lead to an increase in global water usage from 1.1bn to 6.6bn cubic metres by 2027 – in other words, equivalent to more than half of the UK’s total water usage. “Is it really gardeners at fault?” Harry says.

Harry plans to water his plants using a hose, but others have shared how they will more discreetly flout the ban – one TikTok user appears to hide a hosepipe inside a watering can , so a powerful stream emerges from its spout. Some are connecting their hosepipes to buckets being constantly refilled by a running tap to technically remain compliant, despite the large amounts of water still being used.

I speak to Harry in a leafy suburb of Harlow, which is filled with beautiful homes and thriving, well-kept front gardens. Most of the people I meet are planning to comply with the ban, including an elderly woman, whose hands are shaking while she tends a hanging plant with a watering can. She says the ban has “massively impacted her” and she doesn’t want her “beautiful garden” to be ruined.

Isabella, 24, who works in a garden centre, understands why the ban is in place and will comply with it, switching the hose for a watering can and now having to wake up extra early before work. “It takes me 40 minutes to water my plants, whereas it normally takes me 15 minutes,” she says. Some of her customers, who have an exemption from the ban, have told her they “feel like they’re being watched by their neighbours”, especially when tending their front gardens. They are “scared that neighbours will call the police on them so they feel too intimidated to use their hoses”, she says. However, she does understand why her customers “find it frustrating” to restrict their hosepipe use. “It’s just another thing on top of the issues they’re already facing,” she says.

For disabled people, the feeling of being watched and scrutinised is familiar. Maddy Alexander-Grout, a 42-year-old disability advocate and ADHD coach from Southampton, informs people on social media about the benefits and support they might not be aware they are entitled to. She has been dubbed a “sickfluencer” by the tabloids, a label she refutes. “I give disabled people information that they don’t always have access to, because the government doesn’t want people to know about it,” she says.

Anger over the hosepipe ban is the latest culture war furore to target disabled people, despite the fact that the WaterSure tariff – the scheme at the heart of this new controversy – was introduced back in 1999. It comes after a rightwing campaign that has taken aim at Motability, a longstanding scheme that leases 300,000 cars a year to people with mobility problems. A social media and tabloid campaign spread the idea that it was offering disabled people “free” BMWs and Mercedes. In reality , the scheme allows people with serious disabilities to get a car by using a portion of their benefits to pay for a three-year lease, after which the vehicle is returned, with newer cars preferred because they can be resold for a higher price afterwards, saving the taxpayer money.

Following the campaign – which had support from Reform MP Robert Jenrick, who called the scheme “farcical” , and Conservative leader Kemi Badenoch, who said it was a “scam” – Motability was the main target for significant welfare cuts in last year’s autumn budget. Earlier this month, the Treasury said these cuts would “save taxpayers” £1bn by 2030. Rachel Reeves, the then chancellor, denied the campaign was behind the decision. “Disabled people are getting the brunt of everything,” says Grout.

She says there are very valid reasons that disabled people need access to a hosepipe, especially in the scorching heat. Grout has a number of disabilities, including postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome (POTS), which can affect all unconscious bodily functions, including heart rate, blood flow, breathing and digestion; Hashimoto’s disease, an autoimmune condition that can cause extreme fatigue and sensitivity to temperature; and Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, which can cause joint hypermobility. This means she sometimes needs to use a wheelchair.

Grout needs access to a hosepipe to cool down when outdoors because of her Hashimoto’s disease, and to clean her wheelchair, especially when it’s been in a “dusty area and it’s too dirty to bring into the house”. She says other disabled people might need to clean mobility equipment to “wash off bodily fluids” and, for those with respiratory conditions, to clean dusty outside spaces that may cause wheezing.

Although she’s concerned about the rising anger over the ban, she says disabled people should also be sensible and do their bit, where possible, to reduce their water usage. “It’s about common sense, being considerate of others and not rubbing it in people’s faces,” she says. Many disabled people, she adds, “would give up their benefits and a hosepipe to have even a few days where their health wasn’t a problem”.

Laura Greasley is hardly a rule-breaker. She does as much as she can to conserve water, collecting grey water (lightly used water from, say, a bath, shower or washing machine) when possible, and using water butts because “we take individual responsibility really seriously”. However, a pipe outside her home has been leaking for a year. She can hear it when cars drive by, splashing through the wasted water.

Still, her countryside property in the Norfolk Broads is now under a hosepipe ban. After such a long spell of dry weather, her water butt is empty, so instead she has been taking her fill from the untended leak outside her home. She says she “would be happy to support a hosepipe ban under normal circumstances”, but this time she is doing it begrudgingly. “It’s exceptionally frustrating to have leaks outside your house, wasting litres of water, and seeing that it doesn’t appear to be prioritised.”

Her garden is “suffering” as a result of the ban, and her family is “having to work a lot harder” to keep everything alive. “People are extremely frustrated that they’re being asked to sacrifice the gardens they put a lot of time and investment into when the water companies don’t seem to be doing anything to fix these problems. The system is really being run into the ground and people have had enough.”

Vanessa Speight, a water systems professor at the University of Sheffield, says it’s time to replace the ageing, decaying pipes that carry the nation’s water. Of the pipes in our infrastructure that have been examined, the average age is 80. “This means there are some that are a lot older than 80,” she says. Some are weaker than others, particularly those built in the aftermath of the second world war, “when metal was difficult to find”.

Water companies have to justify their spending to Ofwat during the price review process, where the regulator can determine the highest price firms can charge customers. As part of this process, the economic level of leakage is calculated (where the cost of reducing leaks is weighed up against the cost of lost water). The conclusion, Speight says, tends to be that “pipes are very expensive and water is fairly cheap to produce, meaning it’s never going to be economical to replace the pipes”. Privatisation is a factor, too, she adds, leaving the UK in a “difficult situation, where everything about the water systems is monetised or turned into an economic value, but you can’t put a value on water when you run out”.

And running out is a real concern. While the UK’s climate is becoming wetter overall, with winters being defined by punishing rainfall and devastating flooding, our summers are becoming hotter and dryer. The government has been warned that rainwater harvesting , along with other household measures such as taking shorter showers, is needed to avoid severe water shortages in England.

“The distribution of rain is what’s changing, and that’s really hurting the UK, because the reservoir storage that would be required to cope is not there,” says Speight. “We can’t live like a wet country any more; we have to live like a drier country.”

Still, she understands why the hosepipe ban has hit a nerve. “It feels more frustrating as people become aware of water issues and frustrated with the water companies for totally unrelated scenes like wastewater overflows,” she says. Overhauling our water infrastructure will come with a lot of disruption, though. “Unfortunately, it means construction, roadways being disturbed and traffic being diverted. All the things people hate in the short term – but it’s necessary,” she says. And the good news is that if our pipes were fixed, Speight says the need for hosepipe bans “would likely be very small”.

Gardeners are locked in a battle with our changing climate. Some plants that have long been staples in English gardens are now struggling to survive. Tim Parry, a gardener in the Isle of Wight, points to hydrangeas as an example: “They are a very thirsty plant.” He suggests we gravitate towards plants that can tolerate drought, such as Phoenix canariensis (Canary Island date palm) and Trachycarpus fortunei (Chusan palm), both of which require less watering in the long term.

Parry recommends being smart about which plants to water. “Some are a lot hardier than you think, particularly your lawn. It might look dry, brown and horrible right now, but it will come back.”

Nevertheless, it can have a profound impact on people when a plant doesn’t make it. “Plants aren’t just something you stick in your garden,” Parry says, “they have sentimental value. It could be a rose grown in your pot with your grandfather’s ashes, or a tree that you planted in memory of somebody. They’re so important to people.” And more beautiful and meaningful plants could die off as the UK’s climate changes beyond recognition.

“This time round, I feel it’s come on all of a sudden,” says Parry of the drought-like conditions that have scorched gardens. “We’re not talking August. This started happening in late June. I fear for what the future holds.”
