This means she sometimes needs to use a wheelchair.

Grout needs access to a hosepipe to cool down when outdoors because of her Hashimoto’s disease, and to clean her wheelchair, especially when it’s been in a “dusty area and it’s too dirty to bring into the house”. She says other disabled people might need to clean mobility equipment to “wash off bodily fluids” and, for those with respiratory conditions, to clean dusty outside spaces that may cause wheezing.

Although she’s concerned about the rising anger over the ban, she says disabled people should also be sensible and do their bit, where possible, to reduce their water usage. “It’s about common sense, being considerate of others and not rubbing it in people’s faces,” she says. Many disabled people, she adds, “would give up their benefits and a hosepipe to have even a few days where their health wasn’t a problem”.

Laura Greasley is hardly a rule-breaker. She does as much as she can to conserve water, collecting grey water (lightly used water from, say, a bath, shower or washing machine) when possible, and using water butts because “we take individual responsibility really seriously”. However, a pipe outside her home has been leaking for a year. She can hear it when cars drive by, splashing through the wasted water.

Still, her countryside property in the Norfolk Broads is now under a hosepipe ban. After such a long spell of dry weather, her water butt is empty, so instead she has been taking her fill from the untended leak outside her home. She says she “would be happy to support a hosepipe ban under normal circumstances”, but this time she is doing it begrudgingly. “It’s exceptionally frustrating to have leaks outside your house, wasting litres of water, and seeing that it doesn’t appear to be prioritised.”

Her garden is “suffering” as a result of the ban, and her family is “having to work a lot harder” to keep everything alive. “People are extremely frustrated that they’re being asked to sacrifice the gardens they put a lot of time and investment into when the water companies don’t seem to be doing anything to fix these problems. The system is really being run into the ground and people have had enough.”

Vanessa Speight, a water systems professor at the University of Sheffield, says it’s time to replace the ageing, decaying pipes that carry the nation’s water. Of the pipes in our infrastructure that have been examined, the average age is 80. “This means there are some that are a lot older than 80,” she says. Some are weaker than others, particularly those built in the aftermath of the second world war, “when metal was difficult to find”.

Water companies have to justify their spending to Ofwat during the price review process, where the regulator can determine the highest price firms can charge customers. As part of this process, the economic level of leakage is calculated (where the cost of reducing leaks is weighed up against the cost of lost water). The conclusion, Speight says, tends to be that “pipes are very expensive and water is fairly cheap to produce, meaning it’s never going to be economical to replace the pipes”. Privatisation is a factor, too, she adds, leaving the UK in a “difficult situation, where everything about the water systems is monetised or turned into an economic value, but you can’t put a value on water when you run out”.

And running out is a real concern. While the UK’s climate is becoming wetter overall, with winters being defined by punishing rainfall and devastating flooding, our summers are becoming hotter and dryer. The government has been warned that rainwater harvesting , along with other household measures such as taking shorter showers, is needed to avoid severe water shortages in England.

“The distribution of rain is what’s changing, and that’s really hurting the UK, because the reservoir storage that would be required to cope is not there,” says Speight. “We can’t live like a wet country any more; we have to live like a drier country.”

Still, she understands why the hosepipe ban has hit a nerve. “It feels more frustrating as people become aware of water issues and frustrated with the water companies for totally unrelated scenes like wastewater overflows,” she says. Overhauling our water infrastructure will come with a lot of disruption, though. “Unfortunately, it means construction, roadways being disturbed and traffic being diverted. All the things people hate in the short term – but it’s necessary,” she says. And the good news is that if our pipes were fixed, Speight says the need for hosepipe bans “would likely be very small”.

Gardeners are locked in a battle with our changing climate. Some plants that have long been staples in English gardens are now struggling to survive. Tim Parry, a gardener in the Isle of Wight, points to hydrangeas as an example: “They are a very thirsty plant.” He suggests we gravitate towards plants that can tolerate drought, such as Phoenix canariensis (Canary Island date palm) and Trachycarpus fortunei (Chusan palm), both of which require less watering in the long term.
Parry recommends being smart about which plants to water. “Some are a lot hardier than you think, particularly your lawn. It might look dry, brown and horrible right now, but it will come back.”

Nevertheless, it can have a profound impact on people when a plant doesn’t make it. “Plants aren’t just something you stick in your garden,” Parry says, “they have sentimental value. It could be a rose grown in your pot with your grandfather’s ashes, or a tree that you planted in memory of somebody. They’re so important to people.” And more beautiful and meaningful plants could die off as the UK’s climate changes beyond recognition.

“This time round, I feel it’s come on all of a sudden,” says Parry of the drought-like conditions that have scorched gardens. “We’re not talking August. This started happening in late June. I fear for what the future holds.”

articles.title: Hosepipe ban in force for millions of households - is your area affected?
articles.summary: More than eight million households have been placed under hosepipe bans - which has raised questions about how effectively water resources are being managed.
articles.content:
A hosepipe ban for Thames Water customers has come into force bringing the number of people placed under a ban to more than 20 million.

Such measures have been introduced across large areas of southern and eastern England and parts of Wales because successive heatwaves have left water supplies under strain.

But while spring and early summer have been relatively dry for much of the UK - with temperatures regularly exceeding 30C - winter was much wetter than usual.

This means hosepipes cannot be used for watering gardens, washing vehicles and windows and filling pools.

It has raised questions about how effectively water resources are being managed, and whether the UK is prepared for drier summers expected with climate change.

There is no single definition of drought, but it is ultimately caused by a prolonged period of low rainfall, which has knock-on effects for nature, agriculture and water supplies.

Environmental bodies in each UK nation decide whether to declare drought or water scarcity based on an assessment of current water levels and long-term weather forecasts.

Drought is a lack of precipitation, whereas water scarcity is about a mismatch between water supply and demand - so it is not just about the weather.

Large parts of England are currently in "prolonged dry weather" status - the category below drought - according to the Environment Agency.

So far, eight UK water companies have announced hosepipe bans, seven in England and one in Wales.

Prolonged dry weather has been declared in East Anglia, the West Midlands, Devon, Cornwall and the Isles of Scilly, Lincolnshire and Northamptonshire, Hertfordshire and London, Wessex, Thames, and Solent and the South Downs.

The remaining parts of England, mostly in the north, remain in "normal" status.

In Wales, areas across the north and south-east have been escalated from normal to prolonged dry weather status, according to Natural Resources Wales, while there are no official droughts currently in Northern Ireland.

Several areas in eastern Scotland are in "moderate scarcity" but none are in the most extreme category of "significant scarcity".

Our three main sources of water are rivers, reservoirs and from deep underground.

Following the hot, dry weather, river flows were low in June for parts of the UK.

"Without significant rainfall we could see increasing impacts on wildlife, agriculture and water resources as the summer progresses," said Steve Turner, a hydrologist at the UK Centre for Ecology & Hydrology (UKCEH).

Reservoirs are a crucial part of water supplies and levels in Northern Ireland, Wales and Scotland are all "healthy" and above expected for this time of year.

England's reservoirs are below average levels for the time of year, albeit with some variation across the country.

South-east England relies more heavily on groundwater than reservoirs.

Groundwater originates as rainfall and is naturally stored beneath the surface in the pore spaces and fractures in rocks.

In June, groundwater levels were generally at or just below normal, having been topped up by winter rainfall but depleted by a drier spring in most regions.

But water can flow more slowly through some rock types than others, sometimes taking years to fully respond to current conditions.

These groundwater stores "respond more slowly to changes in the climate than rivers, which is why they provide a useful buffer during periods of drought", said Prof Alan MacDonald of the British Geological Survey.

It is why groundwater droughts in the south of England generally take a longer time to develop but can be longer-lasting if they do occur.

Droughts are complex phenomena, driven by a mix of natural and human causes.

The Met Office expects the UK to experience drier summers on average in future as the world warms, though there has been no clear trend so far.

But rising temperatures can play a more direct role by drawing extra moisture from the soil via evaporation.

"A warmer atmosphere is thirstier for moisture and this can mean water in the soil, rivers and reservoirs are depleted more effectively, leading to more rapidly onsetting droughts, heatwaves and wildfires," said Richard Allan, professor of climate science at the University of Reading.

But there are other factors that determine whether dry conditions lead to water shortages, including how we use water.

In a landmark review into the water sector of England and Wales, water companies were criticised for historic underinvestment in water infrastructure.

As part of plans to address water shortages, the government and water companies are planning nine new reservoirs for England by 2050 , in addition to one under construction at Havant Thicket in Hampshire.

But the Environment Agency has warned that measures to tackle water leaks and control water demand - potentially including hosepipe bans and more smart meters - may be needed in England too .

Water companies in Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland - which are all public bodies or non-profit - also said they were taking steps to secure future supplies.

articles.title: Secret court documents reveal potential scale of illegal sewage spilling
articles.summary: The new analysis from a prominent water campaigner emerged after secret court documents were leaked.
articles.content:
Most of the sewage spills into England's waterways in the last two years may have been illegal but gone unpunished, according to new analysis.

UK law states that raw sewage should not be released during "normal" weather conditions, yet until now the regulator has not clarified exactly what that means.

But campaigner Prof Peter Hammond obtained a definition in secret court papers, and said it shows that for more than 80% of the time sewage was being spilled, the weather was "normal", making the spills possibly illegal.

The water companies and the regulator, the Environment Agency (EA), rejected the analysis and said they were working to reduce spill numbers.

Ex-Southern Water boss among four charged over alleged plan to manipulate water tests

The last few years have seen growing awareness and mounting public anger at the amount of raw sewage that is being discharged into England's rivers, lakes and seas.

The country's sewage issues are, in large part, caused by the fact that drains usually take rainfall into the same network of pipes that remove waste from homes.

This means that when it rains, if there is not enough spare capacity, the system and sewage treatment works can be quickly overwhelmed.

But until now, it has not been clear what amount of rainfall there must be before water companies can legally spill raw sewage into waterways. Campaigners have said this has allowed the industry to spill routinely with no consequences.

Earlier this year, a source at the EA sent Prof Hammond undisclosed documents from a court case against Southern Water for illegal sewage spilling in 2021 .

It included a previously undisclosed definition of "heavy rain" conditions. And accompanying internal EA documents confirmed that water companies can spill sewage when there is "heavy" rain i.e. beyond the "normal" weather conditions.

Prof Hammond, a retired academic and campaigner whose work on sewage spills has been cited in Parliament and in economic regulator Ofwat's enforcement decisions, used the definition to run an analysis of water company spill data.

He looked at more than 350 storm overflow outlets in England and whether there had been heavy rain when they were spilling sewage.

The outlets include all of the largest sewage treatment works and a representative selection of smaller ones, across all England's nine water companies serving more than 30m people.

Hammond said he found about 86% of the raw sewage spilling hours took place when there had not been heavy rain, meaning those discharges could be potentially illegal.

None of England's water companies did well in Hammond's analysis, with even the best having a potential illegality rate of over 70% for the last two years.

"It's totally outrageous, both from the point of the water companies who are exploiting the weak regulation but also from the Environment Agency's point of view, where they've not been doing their job properly," Prof Hammond told BBC News.

The EA told BBC News that the "heavy rain" definition Hammond was using was only for the Southern Water sites and that different parts of England had different levels of rainfall, so different thresholds.

Hammond said he had also run his analysis taking into account regional variations in rainfall and it had not significantly changed the level of possible illegality, because most of the spills took place at relatively low levels of rain.

It is not the first time the Environment Agency's enforcement regime has been called into question.

In 2024, the Office for Environmental Protection (OEP), an independent public body, ruled that the EA was not applying legislation properly as to when sewage could be legally discharged. It said the EA was allowing water companies to spill in "normal" weather rather than "exceptional circumstances" - as the law states.

In light of the OEP report the EA issued new guidance last year , external making it clear that spills should not take place during normal weather.

But in an email exchange with the BBC the EA refused to confirm that spills should only take place during "exceptional circumstances", but said it would "always take action" if there had been a breach of the law.

"However, to meet the strict legal requirements needed to pursue a criminal conviction we must consider a wide range of factors including specific site and permit conditions, as well as rainfall totals," its spokesperson said.

Asked to provide details of completed or ongoing enforcement action related to spills in "normal" weather a spokesperson for the EA said they could not, citing "ongoing legal proceedings".

Prof Peter Hammond is a campaigner with Windrush Against Sewage Pollution and has published numerous reports analysing spill data from water companies

In response to Prof Hammond's analysis, a spokesperson for Water UK, which represents the water companies, told the BBC: "As confirmed by the High Court and the Office for Environmental Protection, discharges should only occur in 'exceptional circumstances' or if preventing them is technically possible but would involve excessive cost.

"In any case, we want to end all discharges and are working to end them as fast as we physically can."

articles.title: Possible bloodstains were detected at D4vd’s home, law enforcement testify
articles.summary: Gory details about tarp, mats and inflatable pool come to light during hearing over death of Celeste Rivas Hernandez At singer D4vd’s former Hollywood Hills home, law enforcement detected possible bloodstains – invisible to the naked eye – on items including a green tarp, black rubber mats and a crumpled inflatable pool that had the odor of fresh laundry. The gory details came to light on Wednesday, the second day of a hearing to determine whether there was probable cause for the musician, whose full name is David Anthony Burke, to stand trial for the murder and sexual abuse of Celeste Rivas Hernandez. Continue reading...
articles.content:
Gory details about tarp, mats and inflatable pool come to light during hearing over death of Celeste Rivas Hernandez

At singer D4vd’s former Hollywood Hills home, law enforcement detected possible bloodstains – invisible to the naked eye – on items including a green tarp, black rubber mats and a crumpled inflatable pool that had the odor of fresh laundry.

The gory details came to light on Wednesday, the second day of a hearing to determine whether there was probable cause for the musician, whose full name is David Anthony Burke, to stand trial for the murder and sexual abuse of Celeste Rivas Hernandez.

Prosecutors accuse Burke of stabbing Rivas Hernandez, 14, to death at his Los Angeles residence to silence her from divulging the sexual nature of their relationship and jeopardizing his skyrocketing career.

Burke has pleaded not guilty to charges that include first-degree murder, “lewd and lascivious acts” with a child under the age of 14, and the mutilation of human remains. His defense team has said their client “was not the cause” of Rivas Hernandez’s death.

Last September, Lauren Wallace, a criminalist with the Los Angeles police department, found two chainsaws in the garage of a Hollywood Hills home where the Houston-raised musician had been living. The chainsaws tested negative for possible blood.

Rubber mats covering a garage floor, however, likely had blood stains, Wallace testified. Linear cuts, consistent with that of a sharp object, were found on the mats.

Wallace also observed damage to the pool. “It appeared that it had been cut,” she said.

Chelsea Murillo, another criminalist in LAPD’s forensic science division, examined the 2023 black Tesla Model X that contained Rivas Hernandez’s remains.

A layer of dust coated the vehicle’s exterior, Murillo said on Wednesday, which suggested it had been outside for a prolonged period. In the front trunk, she observed maggots and flies. A strong odor that resembled a decomposing body came from the car, she said, even amid multiple air fresheners. Possible traces of blood were found in the Tesla’s third row.

Defense attorney Blair Berk cast skepticism over the findings of the criminalists’ forensic testing.

It’s “only presumptive. It doesn’t mean you found blood,” Berk asked during her cross-examination of Murillo.

The scope of the singer’s earnings also came into clear view on Wednesday, as prosecutors sought to establish the musician had financial motivation to kill Rivas Hernandez. Burke had a multimillion-dollar recording deal, an executive at his former label, Interscope, testified.

From 2022 to 2025, Interscope paid Burke and affiliated entities more than $7.2m, a figure that encompassed legal fees and a trust mandated by California law for underage entertainers, according to a summary of payments exhibited to the courtroom.

Details of Burke’s compensation followed a testy exchange in the morning, as prosecutors countered the defense’s suggestion that their client was not privy to his finances.

Attorneys for Burke repeatedly objected to questions from deputy district attorney Beth Silverman about whether the singer’s former financial manager, Benjamin Gregor, knew of Burke arranging tickets for Rivas Hernandez to travel to London and his home in Texas.

Rivas Hernandez’s parents, Jesus Rivas and Mercedes Martinez, returned to court on Wednesday. Dressed in all black, the two sat in the first row, accompanied by their lawyer.

Martinez dropped her head and wiped her eyes in the early afternoon, when prosecutors presented a photograph of her daughter’s passport, which a Caltrans employee discovered in January 2026 in a dirt embankment off State Route 154 in Santa Barbara.

“Once he found the card, he Googled the name to try and see if it was someone that lived locally in the area. He began seeing articles and news stories and when he realized what he had found, he called our dispatch,” Sean Buenrostro, a California highway patrol officer, said on the stand.

At the hearing’s start, prosecutors showed graphic photographs of Rivas Hernandez’s dismembered body and alleged the singer had severely mutilated her remains. They also presented evidence they say shows the singer used his credit cards to purchase chainsaws, a body bag and a shovel.

Burke, now 21, was arrested in April and has been held in custody since then without bail. He has appeared in court in an orange jumpsuit and thick black glasses.

If convicted of murder, he could face the death penalty, or life in prison without the possibility of parole.

Prosecutors have not yet indicated whether they will pursue capital punishment.

After court concluded on Wednesday, Patrick Steinfeld, an attorney for the Rivas Hernandez family, spoke to reporters outside.

“It’s a hard thing for the family. They are very private individuals and they’re doing the best they can,” he said in response to a question about how Rivas Hernandez’s parents reacted to the evidence presented about the Tesla.

“They have a hole in their heart that will never be replaced,” he said.
paragraphs: Gory details about tarp, mats and inflatable pool come to light during hearing over death of Celeste Rivas Hernandez

At singer D4vd’s former Hollywood Hills home, law enforcement detected possible bloodstains – invisible to the naked eye – on items including a green tarp, black rubber mats and a crumpled inflatable pool that had the odor of fresh laundry.

The gory details came to light on Wednesday, the second day of a hearing to determine whether there was probable cause for the musician, whose full name is David Anthony Burke, to stand trial for the murder and sexual abuse of Celeste Rivas Hernandez.

Prosecutors accuse Burke of stabbing Rivas Hernandez, 14, to death at his Los Angeles residence to silence her from divulging the sexual nature of their relationship and jeopardizing his skyrocketing career.

Burke has pleaded not guilty to charges that include first-degree murder, “lewd and lascivious acts” with a child under the age of 14, and the mutilation of human remains. His defense team has said their client “was not the cause” of Rivas Hernandez’s death.

Last September, Lauren Wallace, a criminalist with the Los Angeles police department, found two chainsaws in the garage of a Hollywood Hills home where the Houston-raised musician had been living. The chainsaws tested negative for possible blood.
Rubber mats covering a garage floor, however, likely had blood stains, Wallace testified. Linear cuts, consistent with that of a sharp object, were found on the mats.
Wallace also observed damage to the pool. “It appeared that it had been cut,” she said.
Chelsea Murillo, another criminalist in LAPD’s forensic science division, examined the 2023 black Tesla Model X that contained Rivas Hernandez’s remains.

A layer of dust coated the vehicle’s exterior, Murillo said on Wednesday, which suggested it had been outside for a prolonged period. In the front trunk, she observed maggots and flies. A strong odor that resembled a decomposing body came from the car, she said, even amid multiple air fresheners. Possible traces of blood were found in the Tesla’s third row.
Defense attorney Blair Berk cast skepticism over the findings of the criminalists’ forensic testing.
It’s “only presumptive. It doesn’t mean you found blood,” Berk asked during her cross-examination of Murillo.

The scope of the singer’s earnings also came into clear view on Wednesday, as prosecutors sought to establish the musician had financial motivation to kill Rivas Hernandez. Burke had a multimillion-dollar recording deal, an executive at his former label, Interscope, testified.

From 2022 to 2025, Interscope paid Burke and affiliated entities more than $7.2m, a figure that encompassed legal fees and a trust mandated by California law for underage entertainers, according to a summary of payments exhibited to the courtroom.
Details of Burke’s compensation followed a testy exchange in the morning, as prosecutors countered the defense’s suggestion that their client was not privy to his finances.

Attorneys for Burke repeatedly objected to questions from deputy district attorney Beth Silverman about whether the singer’s former financial manager, Benjamin Gregor, knew of Burke arranging tickets for Rivas Hernandez to travel to London and his home in Texas.
Rivas Hernandez’s parents, Jesus Rivas and Mercedes Martinez, returned to court on Wednesday. Dressed in all black, the two sat in the first row, accompanied by their lawyer.

Martinez dropped her head and wiped her eyes in the early afternoon, when prosecutors presented a photograph of her daughter’s passport, which a Caltrans employee discovered in January 2026 in a dirt embankment off State Route 154 in Santa Barbara.

“Once he found the card, he Googled the name to try and see if it was someone that lived locally in the area. He began seeing articles and news stories and when he realized what he had found, he called our dispatch,” Sean Buenrostro, a California highway patrol officer, said on the stand.

At the hearing’s start, prosecutors showed graphic photographs of Rivas Hernandez’s dismembered body and alleged the singer had severely mutilated her remains. They also presented evidence they say shows the singer used his credit cards to purchase chainsaws, a body bag and a shovel.
Burke, now 21, was arrested in April and has been held in custody since then without bail. He has appeared in court in an orange jumpsuit and thick black glasses.
If convicted of murder, he could face the death penalty, or life in prison without the possibility of parole.
Prosecutors have not yet indicated whether they will pursue capital punishment.
After court concluded on Wednesday, Patrick Steinfeld, an attorney for the Rivas Hernandez family, spoke to reporters outside.

“It’s a hard thing for the family. They are very private individuals and they’re doing the best they can,” he said in response to a question about how Rivas Hernandez’s parents reacted to the evidence presented about the Tesla.
“They have a hole in their heart that will never be replaced,” he said.

articles.title: Chasing new skills, going back to basics and pushing for collective action: how software engineers are adapting to AI
articles.summary: Software engineering was one of the best-paying professions in the US in 2022, but the advent of AI has disrupted it, leading to several layoffs and underemployment Every weekday, Matt, a software engineer, looks forward to his four-hour train commute to Pawling, New York. It’s time he uses to work on his own project: a browser-based video game for which he writes every line of code himself. “I am actively trying to keep my axe sharp,” said Matt, who did not want to use his actual name, to protect his employment. In the last six months, Matt’s job has increasingly shifted away from coding, problem solving and software architecture towards reviewing code generated by artificial intelligence. Convinced that the shift will weaken his skills, he’s doing what he can to keep them intact. “I am trying not to leverage AI where I can.” Continue reading...
articles.content:
Software engineering was one of the best-paying professions in the US in 2022, but the advent of AI has disrupted it, leading to several layoffs and underemployment

Every weekday, Matt, a software engineer, looks forward to his four-hour train commute to Pawling, New York. It’s time he uses to work on his own project: a browser-based video game for which he writes every line of code himself.

“I am actively trying to keep my axe sharp,” said Matt, who did not want to use his actual name, to protect his employment. In the last six months, Matt’s job has increasingly shifted away from coding, problem solving and software architecture towards reviewing code generated by artificial intelligence. Convinced that the shift will weaken his skills, he’s doing what he can to keep them intact. “I am trying not to leverage AI where I can.”

His career as a software engineer, which typically has paid more than $200,000 annually, used to feel like a sure thing. But after a layoff last summer and a warning from his current boss to use AI more, he said his future feels dark.

For a generation of workers like Matt, software engineering promised stability, security and upward mobility. But as AI changes how software is developed – Google says 75% of its code is now written by AI – it’s altering the profession much faster than the rank-and-file anticipated. Software engineers are frustrated, anxious and trying to adapt to a startling new reality in which the value of their skills is unclear. As a result, they’re doubling down on fundamentals, chasing new skills to stay relevant, seeking collective action to push for better protections or even contemplating exiting the industry entirely, according to more than a dozen software engineers who spoke to the Guardian.

Software engineering was one of the biggest and best-paying professions in the US in 2022, with 1.5 million practitioners earning twice the national median salary, according to the US Bureau of Labor Statistics. Pay had risen amid escalating talent wars , during which companies offered bonuses of up to hundreds of thousands of dollars to snag and retain top programmers. Last year, nearly 50 million people worldwide worked as developers, according to the market research firm SlashData.

But since the release of OpenAI’s ChatGPT in 2022, more than 600,000 US tech workers have lost their jobs, according to the tech layoff tracker Layoff.fyi. Meanwhile, the unemployment rate for computer science graduates rose to 7% in 2024, up from 6.1% the previous year, and their underemployment rate was more than 19%, data from the New York Fed shows. US tech job postings on Indeed also dropped 36% from 2020 to 2025 .

Experts are unsure what’s next for software engineers, but they agree on one thing: coding skills may be losing value, but the ability to evaluate AI-written code is becoming more important.

“It’s hard to say what exactly the profession will look like in two years, but it’s clear that the skill of writing code is over,” Bouke Klein Teeselink, assistant professor of economics at King’s College London, told the Guardian. “AI is hugely augmenting what it means to be a software engineer, and the reasonable yardstick” for success depends on how well engineers use the tech.

Software engineers still have a job to do, AI has just changed it, said Ethan Mollick, an associate professor of management at the University of Pennsylvania’s Wharton School and author of the upcoming book Co-Existence .

“Now it’s not about who can write the most code,” he said. Instead, focus is on defining problems, designing systems and directing AI tools effectively, he said. “It shifts the skills around, so suddenly that’s where the value is.”

Before AI, Matt said he was a “leading voice” in a solution’s execution, but now “the line between what I am deciding and what AI provides has certainly blurred.”

Software engineers are grappling with a daunting choice: soldier on in an increasingly unpredictable industry or seek an alternative path.

George Dover, a software engineer of six years in Portland, Oregon, became a substitute kindergarten teacher while he searched for new roles after a layoff at Inuit Mailchimp in late 2024.

“It’s very difficult to let go of something that was a large part of your personality for a good number of years,” he said. “What else is there for me?”

But Dover didn’t give up. Realizing the need to understand AI, he used AI to generate code to build websites and then evaluated it to learn its strengths and limitations. He checked the code for errors, redundancies, unusual AI decisions, bugs and visual glitches.

“The quality has to be rigorously tested,” he said. “Sometimes that tradeoff is a good one, other times it leads you down rabbit holes that take longer than coding it yourself.”

It paid off. Almost two years after being laid off, 400 applications and several interviews later, Dover landed a software engineering job – one oriented towards AI.

Dover isn’t the only one experimenting. More non-technical workers are writing code, expanding overall output, which could drive demand for software engineers, said Teeselink. Validating AI-written code requires someone to “find vulnerabilities, understand errors, check security, etc., which non-coders will not be able to do”, he said.

But it’s too early to make any definitive declarations about the profession, especially since AI only started generating good quality code last year, said Shriram Krishnamurthi, professor of computer science at Brown University. Still, the growing need for code reviews will probably weed out some professionals, he said.

“Some software engineers trained well for this, and many did not,” he said. “Those who did will thrive; those who didn’t are going to have to retool.”

Human software engineers will still be needed if for no other reason than the cost of AI, said David Malan, a Harvard University computer science professor. In their race to build and run models, OpenAI reportedly spent $8bn and Anthropic is expected to have burnt $3bn last year, according to Reuters. Costs are widely expected to eventually pass on to customers. So rather than companies relying entirely on the tech, Malan expects to see a “healthier balance of software engineers being supported by AI”.

In December 2013, President Barack Obama launched a $4bn initiative called Computer Science for All to teach computer science to all American students, calling it the “new basic” skill for economic opportunity. The private sector echoed a similar message, with Mark Zuckerberg and Bill Gates participating in coding tutorial videos for the educational non-profit CodeAI. The same year, coding bootcamps exploded, producing more than 2,000 graduates, which rose more than 1000% by 2020, according to coding bootcamp tracking organization Course Report.

That’s why Los Angeles resident Sam, who asked for anonymity to protect his employment, pivoted to software engineering a decade ago. After dropping out of school for a music degree, racking up $130,000 in student loans, and taking a stab at some odd jobs, he pursued a software engineering degree.

But in his first five years on the job, he’s gone from feeling professionally stable to fearing he could lose it all. He hates that AI has taken over “the creative, fun part of the job”, and reduced it down to the worst part: “reviewing code I didn’t write”, said Sam.

He’s worried about the future, fearing a layoff would end his career by forcing him to compete with displaced talent from Google, Amazon and Netflix, he said.

“I’m thinking as I sit in my office, ‘What if I opened a food truck? What if I got into forestry?’” he added.

Sam isn’t alone in his malaise. More than a decade after the coding push, the buzz for the profession is fading. Enrollment in computer and information science programs at four-year universities fell 8.1% and graduate enrollment dropped 14% in the 2025-2026 school year, according to the National Student Clearinghouse, a non-profit that tracks educational institutions.

As AI threatens their livelihoods, software engineers are increasingly organizing and banding together.

For Kaitlin Cort, watching AI reshape her role led her to quit and start a resource center for tech workers caught in the industry’s disruption.

“I’m not a very senior-level engineer,” said Cort, who has eight years of software engineering experience. “I can see that the pace at which AI was getting better was faster than the pace at which I was getting better. That definitely made me really anxious.”

Cort originally became a software engineer to pay off student loan debt. She also taught at coding bootcamps, where students from low-income backgrounds and domestic violence shelters showed her firsthand what coding could do for people’s lives. But once her job shifted from writing code to reviewing what AI generated, she began to rethink her career and where she might make a greater impact.

In February, Cort started What We Will, which is part of the Tech Workers Coalition and helps workers in the industry navigate layoffs and negotiations, access basic income in unemployment, up-skill and organize unions. The center’s inaugural campaign, launched in its first month, aimed to help Amazon workers organize against rapid AI adoption and guide laid-off workers through their benefits. Its second campaign targeted workers cut from Oracle, aiding them with severance negotiation and creating support groups. In May, it worked with Meta workers to discuss employee surveillance.

Worries about AI and mass layoffs are driving membership and interest in collective action, Cort said. Whenever What We Will makes mass outreach calls, more than 100 people join within a week’s time, she said. Additionally, at least 10 workers reach out each day to organize through the Tech Workers Coalition.

But she’s largely building without a roadmap since few in the industry have done this before. As a result, she’s turned to Alphabet Workers Union and the Washington Post Tech Guild, among others, for guidance.

“There’s a lot of need right now,” she said. “We just, as an industry, don’t have a guild – we don’t have regulations or standards that are really shared.”

articles.title: Rugby players seek new lawyers for brain injury cases against governing bodies
articles.summary: Richard Boardman was criticised by the managing judge Players want ‘firm with resources and specialist expertise’ Hundreds of former rugby league and rugby union players taking legal action against the game’s governing bodies are now “staring into the precipice”, according to the barrister representing the claimants, Susan Rodway KC. Richard Boardman, the solicitor who has been the organising force behind the litigation, has requested formally to come off the record after his handling of the case was criticised by the managing judge, Senior Master Cook. The case may now be taken over by Leigh Day, who has been working on it as consultants since February. Continue reading...
articles.content:
Hundreds of former rugby league and rugby union players taking legal action against the game’s governing bodies are now “staring into the precipice”, according to the barrister representing the claimants, Susan Rodway KC.

Richard Boardman, the solicitor who has been the organising force behind the litigation, has requested formally to come off the record after his handling of the case was criticised by the managing judge, Senior Master Cook. The case may now be taken over by Leigh Day, who has been working on it as consultants since February.

The players issued a statement on Wednesday night that they had decided to “terminate the instruction of Rylands Garth” and that the process of appointing new solicitors is under way. “This decision has not been taken lightly. We would like to place on record our sincere gratitude to Rylands Garth and in particular Richard Boardman,” the statement said.

“We concluded it was in the best interests of the claimants to appoint a firm with resources and specialist expertise required to lead the litigation through its next phase. We would like to reassure the court, our fellow claimants and the wider rugby family that we believe this represents a positive step in progressing the litigation.”

Cook described it as a “dramatic change”. It comes as the claimants face the threat of strikeouts because of Rylands Garth’s failure to comply with the judge’s order to disclose to the defendants all available medical documents. If the strikeouts go ahead, then hundreds of claimants will be removed from the proceedings.

“There is a sword hanging over these players,” Rodway said, as she appealed for relief from the strikeouts. “Although the issues are generic the impact of them relates to distinct individuals who have suffered life‑changing injuries, and who have suffered enormous stress and anxiety.”

Some, Rodway said, have taken their own lives. “These are not claims for double glazing gone wrong, but for catastrophic brain injuries and each of these players has a claim for hundreds of thousands, if not millions, of pounds.”

According to Boardman’s court filings, Leigh Day is “engaging in a period of due diligence in order to decide whether they would be in a position to take over subject to the clients’ agreement”. A spokesperson for Leigh Day said the firm “have not had an opportunity to properly consider whether they can come on record as solicitors in this claim” and that they “are reviewing the position”.

The claimants argue “the change of solicitor on the record not only demonstrates how seriously the claimants take the criticisms levelled at them, but that there will be a completely different regime going forward in which both the court and the defendants can have full confidence”.

Confidence seems to be in very short supply. For the past two years, both cases, which are still in the pre-trial phase, have been held up by a series of arguments about whether or not Boardman’s firm, Rylands Garth, have complied with their disclosure obligations.

The defendants – World Rugby, the Rugby Football Union , the Welsh Rugby Union in one case, and the Rugby Football League and the British Amateur Rugby League Association in the other – requested originally to be allowed to conduct their own medical testing on the claimants. It was decided by the court this full medical testing would be “complex, expensive, and unnecessary” and that the claimants would be obliged to disclose all medical records in their control to the defendants.

The two legal teams would then be able to select their own pools of proposed lead claimants from among the hundreds involved. There has been little progress in this because, the defendants argue, Rylands Garth has failed repeatedly to provide all the documents.

Cook was unequivocal that he agrees with them on this. He said he felt “at a loss to understand” Boardman’s position. “On any view there has been widespread non-compliance and there has been late compliance.” He also accused Boardman of “providing misleading evidence in court”.

“We’ve been four years trying to get to this point,” he said. “It has required orders and those orders have had to be backed up by an Unless Order, and even that wasn’t accepted, but appealed against.”

Cook did not agree with the claimants’ argument that the defendants were pettifogging them. “It’s not the defendants being difficult for the sake of being difficult, it is the defendants wanting to make sure this process has integrity we can all rely on. We’re here to do justice to people, but the process has to have integrity.”

Rodway argued the claimants “are altering the process and will have a different person at the head” and that “the change of solicitors is significantly important because it shows the players taking matters into their own hands”. Leigh Day recently won a case against British Gymnastics over abuse allegations in the past. It is understood that Boardman intends to remain involved in the case as co-counsel.

Cook described it as a “dramatic change”. It comes as the claimants face the threat of strikeouts because of Rylands Garth’s failure to comply with the judge’s order to disclose to the defendants all available medical documents. If the strikeouts go ahead, then hundreds of claimants will be removed from the proceedings.

“There is a sword hanging over these players,” Rodway said, as she appealed for relief from the strikeouts. “Although the issues are generic the impact of them relates to distinct individuals who have suffered life‑changing injuries, and who have suffered enormous stress and anxiety.”

Some, Rodway said, have taken their own lives. “These are not claims for double glazing gone wrong, but for catastrophic brain injuries and each of these players has a claim for hundreds of thousands, if not millions, of pounds.”

According to Boardman’s court filings, Leigh Day is “engaging in a period of due diligence in order to decide whether they would be in a position to take over subject to the clients’ agreement”. A spokesperson for Leigh Day said the firm “have not had an opportunity to properly consider whether they can come on record as solicitors in this claim” and that they “are reviewing the position”.

The claimants argue “the change of solicitor on the record not only demonstrates how seriously the claimants take the criticisms levelled at them, but that there will be a completely different regime going forward in which both the court and the defendants can have full confidence”.

Confidence seems to be in very short supply. For the past two years, both cases, which are still in the pre-trial phase, have been held up by a series of arguments about whether or not Boardman’s firm, Rylands Garth, have complied with their disclosure obligations.

The defendants – World Rugby, the Rugby Football Union , the Welsh Rugby Union in one case, and the Rugby Football League and the British Amateur Rugby League Association in the other – requested originally to be allowed to conduct their own medical testing on the claimants. It was decided by the court this full medical testing would be “complex, expensive, and unnecessary” and that the claimants would be obliged to disclose all medical records in their control to the defendants.

The two legal teams would then be able to select their own pools of proposed lead claimants from among the hundreds involved. There has been little progress in this because, the defendants argue, Rylands Garth has failed repeatedly to provide all the documents.

Cook was unequivocal that he agrees with them on this. He said he felt “at a loss to understand” Boardman’s position. “On any view there has been widespread non-compliance and there has been late compliance.” He also accused Boardman of “providing misleading evidence in court”.

“We’ve been four years trying to get to this point,” he said. “It has required orders and those orders have had to be backed up by an Unless Order, and even that wasn’t accepted, but appealed against.”

Cook did not agree with the claimants’ argument that the defendants were pettifogging them. “It’s not the defendants being difficult for the sake of being difficult, it is the defendants wanting to make sure this process has integrity we can all rely on. We’re here to do justice to people, but the process has to have integrity.”

Rodway argued the claimants “are altering the process and will have a different person at the head” and that “the change of solicitors is significantly important because it shows the players taking matters into their own hands”. Leigh Day recently won a case against British Gymnastics over abuse allegations in the past. It is understood that Boardman intends to remain involved in the case as co-counsel.

articles.title: Teenager drops social media addiction lawsuit against Meta
articles.summary: Claims from a 15-year-old boy were set to go to trial next week in Los Angeles, but the case has now been dropped.
articles.content:
Teens and parents have increasingly claimed ill-effects of social media use.

A Florida teenager who sued Meta claiming that he was addicted to and harmed by his use of platforms, such as Instagram, has dropped his case.

The 15-year-old plaintiff, known by the initials R.K.C., had in recent weeks settled the same claims against TikTok, Snapchat, and YouTube, all of which have been accused in a large lawsuit of creating addictive platforms harmful to children.

R.K.C.'s claims were to go before a Los Angeles jury next week as part of court proceedings that have seen Meta and YouTube lose once already .

A Meta spokeswoman told the BBC: "The claims never held up, and this outcome makes clear that we will not back away from defending ourselves against baseless lawsuits."

Lawyers for R.K.C., Emily Jeffcott and Rahul Ravipudi, told the BBC that the young plaintiff "came into this process wanting to hold social media companies accountable and push for changes to protect young people like himself".

The lawyers noted concerns about a jury trial stretching on for weeks and said R.K.C. was "ready to close this chapter and focus on his recovery and engage in therapy as he aspires to have a normal life".

The Meta spokeswoman said he received no payment for dropping his claims.

R.K.C. had claimed that common social media features such as infinite scroll and autoplay, both of which continuously and automatically show users new content on a platform, drove compulsive use that became a type of addiction.

He argued it caused him anxiety and sleep deprivation, among other issues.

Meta and YouTube faced similar accusations from a young woman during the initial trial.

A jury ultimately found them liable for harming her mental health and were told to pay her $6mn (£4.4mn) in damages.

It was the first time that social media platforms had been held liable for their effects on users.

The Los Angeles Superior Court is overseeing hundreds of lawsuits making similar claims of addiction and harm against social media companies. A small number of cases have been selected to go to trial.

Across the US, there are thousands of lawsuits accusing Meta and other major social media platforms of harming young children.

In May, Meta settled for an undisclosed amount with a school district that had sued it for negatively impacting students, so much so that schools incurred costs related to student welfare. The school district had sought $60m in damages.

That settlement came not long after a New Mexico judge told Meta to pay $375m for misleading users on the safety of its platforms for children.

articles.title: Charles Gaines, whose book Pumping Iron helped to popularize bodybuilding, dies at 84
articles.summary: 1974 book led to making of seminal documentary and introduced Arnold Schwarzenegger to the world Charles Gaines, the author whose book and documentary Pumping Iron helped bring professional bodybuilding into the mainstream and introduce Arnold Schwarzenegger to the world, has died. He was 84. Gaines died on Tuesday, according to Greta Gaines, the author’s daughter. She did not reveal a cause. Continue reading...
articles.content:
1974 book led to making of seminal documentary and introduced Arnold Schwarzenegger to the world

Charles Gaines, the author whose book and documentary Pumping Iron helped bring professional bodybuilding into the mainstream and introduce Arnold Schwarzenegger to the world, has died. He was 84.

Gaines died on Tuesday, according to Greta Gaines, the author’s daughter. She did not reveal a cause.

“You always marched to your own drummer and inspired everyone around you to do the same,” she wrote in a tribute to her father. “Follow that bliss, catch that fish and kiss those lips while you can because my father was the live life to the fullest kinda guy.”

Gaines collaborated with George Butler to write Pumping Iron: The Art and Sport of Bodybuilding, which led to the making of the documentary Pumping Iron, for which Butler and Gaines wrote the script.

In the introduction to their 1974 book, Gaines wrote that he and Butler considered it “a sort of respectful report from the interior of an interesting, colorful and heretofore ignored region of American life”.

In a 2022 edition, he admitted that neither author thought “many people outside of the bodybuilding world would be interested in” the book. Gaines wrote that Schwarzenegger almost single-handedly “brought bodybuilding out of the shadows it had languished in since its earliest days in America and into the limelight”.

Schwarzenegger, who won seven Mr Olympia and five Mr Universe titles and who was pictured flexing on the cover of Pumping Iron, harnessed the attention into a career, first in films – including The Terminator, True Lies, Twins and Predator – and then politics, becoming governor of California from 2003 to 2011.

Gaines wrote that he had been involved in bodybuilding in one way or another since he was 16, when he began lifting weights. Pumping Iron began as an article for Sports Illustrated in 1972 on a bodybuilding contest for Mr East Coast.

“If we felt at times a little like 19th century explorers, it was because we found bodybuilding to be as primeval and unmapped as parts of Labrador,” he wrote. “Nobody, we discovered, had been back into it to send out a report on what it was like.”

Gaines and Butler used the book as the basis for their 1977 documentary Pumping Iron, which centers on the rivalry between Schwarzenegger and fellow bodybuilder Lou Ferrigno in the competition for a Mr Olympia title. Ferrigno later starred on TV in The Incredible Hulk.

Gaines was raised in Alabama and earned a bachelor’s degree from Birmingham-Southern College and a master of fine arts in writing from the University of Iowa.

Gaines published numerous books throughout his career, including Waters Far and Near: Tales of Angling Adventure and Misadventure Around the World and the novels Stay Hungry, Dangler and Survival Games. Stay Hungry was adapted into a movie starring Jeff Bridges, giving Schwarzenegger his first movie role.

“He climbed mountains, trekked across ice, fished, and hunted on almost every continent, writing about every adventure in a way that captured people’s hearts and minds. He was interested in everything – and his interest was contagious,” Schwarzenegger wrote on Instagram.

“I’m lucky that he was always looking for new frontiers, because that’s how he found me and our niche sport of bodybuilding.”

In addition to his daughter Greta, Gaines is survived by his wife, the artist Patricia Ellisor Gaines, and two other children, Latham and Shelby Gaines.

articles.title: Cutting down on ultra-processed foods isn’t easy. Here are eight tips that worked for my family
articles.summary: Experts say reducing ultra-processed foods can be challenging in a world built around convenience, but these strategies can help make it more manageable I have been trying to eat fewer ultra-processed foods with my family for five years, and it has been challenging. Many experts agree that ultra-processed foods (UPF) pose health risks, whether because of unhealthy ingredients , links to disease and environmental impacts , or replacing more nutritious foods. Thus, some consumers – including me – are interested in removing them from their daily diet. Learn techniques: “Once you know how to saute, it doesn’t matter what you put in the pan.” Develop knife skills: “If people want to get more comfortable in the kitchen, they should take a knife skills class. Those skills are a massive upscale for confidence in the kitchen.” Know your taste: “Know what you like, then create that, because that’s the starting place. Don’t start by making miso brownies when the core is to make a really good brownie that you like, because then you’ll do it again.” Continue reading...
articles.content:
Experts say reducing ultra-processed foods can be challenging in a world built around convenience, but these strategies can help make it more manageable

I have been trying to eat fewer ultra-processed foods with my family for five years, and it has been challenging.

Many experts agree that ultra-processed foods (UPF) pose health risks, whether because of unhealthy ingredients , links to disease and environmental impacts , or replacing more nutritious foods. Thus, some consumers – including me – are interested in removing them from their daily diet.

Getting here took time, effort and consistency, but we have had some success. Here are some strategies we used in our family.

Plant foods are some of the easiest, most plentiful substitutes for UPF. Most obvious are fruits and vegetables, but nuts, seeds, popcorn and roasted seaweed are other tasty, nutritious options.

When it comes to introducing fruits and vegetables, the six to 12-month age is crucial, says Kary Rappaport, pediatric occupational therapist and the executive director of Solid Starts , an organization that supports parents with feeding their children: “It is an exceedingly important window of time, when most babies are most developmentally open to a wide variety of foods and flavors.”

Introducing new foods to older children is similar: use small tastes, different preparations and a child’s natural curiosity, she says. Focus on describing the food, rather than whether the child likes it – for example, encourage them to lick or take a bite and notice qualities like sweetness or temperature. “It’s not a value judgment on the food,” she says.

Additionally, timing matters. “We are all more motivated to try and like things when we’re a little bit hungry,” says Rappaport. Try “putting out a tray of raw veggies with hummus while you’re cooking dinner, during that window where kids are looking to snack”, she suggests. On the other hand, don’t try to force a hangry child to try new veggies.

“Even when you don’t have great options that are fully non-UPF, incorporate vegetables and fruits and raw products as much as possible,” says Julia Wolfson, an associate professor in public health at Johns Hopkins University.

At our local farmers’ market, there is a stall selling fantastic apples. When in season, they are consistently crisp, sweet and apple-y. This is true for much of the food at the market.

“Whatever they have in your farmers’ market is what is seasonal, and what is seasonal is going to be better,” says chef Penny Stankiewicz. “If you go to a standard American grocery store and you buy a perfectly round, somewhat red tomato, it’s going to have absolutely no flavor in January. But in August, when it’s prime tomato season, you don’t have to even do anything to that tomato to make it amazing.”

Produce is typically more expensive at a farmer’s market, and not everyone has access to one. But the flavor payoff can be huge. Otherwise, try the produce at different grocery stores; additionally, frozen and canned produce is usually preserved at its peak. After you source your produce, learn the best ways to store it so it lasts as long as possible.

Part of my motivation to minimize UPF was not wanting to support powerful corporations that exploited workers, mistreated animals and harmed ecosystems. The more I learned about food systems, the less I wanted to participate in them as a consumer.

Teaching children where food comes from and how to prepare it builds appreciation and understanding. Rappaport also suggests discussing how foods make their bodies feel: for instance, “if I eat too many gummy worms or I only eat a bag of Flamin’ Hot Cheetos on our car trip, I’m going to feel bad. It’s not gonna sit well in my belly.”

Familiarize yourself and your family with the definition of UPF, so that you can identify one by looking at an ingredient list, says Wolfson.

I already knew how to cook, but I studied the science of cooking as we embarked on this project. Now, my from-scratch meals taste better than before, sometimes more so than UPF alternatives.

Often the barriers to cooking are systemic: insufficient time and energy to cook. People may also lack confidence in their kitchen skills. “We need to make it easier for people to cook healthy meals from scratch, and that means making those scratch ingredients more available and affordable, and building those skills from a young age so that people have the confidence and self-efficacy,” says Wolfson.

Learn techniques: “Once you know how to saute, it doesn’t matter what you put in the pan.”

Develop knife skills: “If people want to get more comfortable in the kitchen, they should take a knife skills class. Those skills are a massive upscale for confidence in the kitchen.”

Know your taste: “Know what you like, then create that, because that’s the starting place. Don’t start by making miso brownies when the core is to make a really good brownie that you like, because then you’ll do it again.”

Wolfson found in her research that “when people cook more at home, they consume fewer ultra-processed foods.” She notes that even when people are cooking frequently, the majority of their calories often still come from UPF. Try not to feel defeated if you notice something similar; this speaks to the ubiquity of UPF, she says: “If you picture a typical American grocery store, almost 70% of the foods in the store are ultra processed … that’s what’s mostly available to us.”

Stankiewicz says to approach baking with fearlessness. “Messing up is part of it and is really how you learn,” she says, telling me that brownies were created by accident when a baker didn’t add enough flour to the cake.

The next step is to find trusted recipe sources. Start with recipes that are not overly intimidating or complicated. Read the recipe through before beginning, then gather the ingredients. Organize the ingredients (mise-en-place) – and then just do it!

Consuming UPF is easy. It takes very little time and mental capacity to, say, unwrap and heat something. Try to make cooking and baking at home similarly easy.

First, master a few dishes. “Find a handful of recipes or techniques that are accessible in terms of ingredients, cost, number of ingredients, and how long they’re going to take to make. Then just get really good at those,” says Stankiewicz.

Second, build a pantry. Keep a stock of frequently used ingredients. My husband and I maintain a shared shopping list, and if we notice something running low, we update the list.

Third, make more than you need. Leftovers are great. Even better are meals that can be frozen and reheated because your past self did all the work.

We have a rule at the table: no phones or toys during mealtimes. We have conversations instead (often mundane and not terribly deep). Our meals aren’t long; they usually take 20 minutes from when we sit down to when my youngest is done.

“The research is really clear that family meals, where people are eating together, have a huge positive impact on child development across many different areas in terms of school performance, mental health and social engagement,” says Rappaport.

It can be difficult when schedules are varied. Rappaport has older children and strives to fit in three dinners together each week around work and extracurriculars. It’s less about “having a Norman Rockwell meal at the dinner table” than finding opportunities to engage, she says. “It can be burritos that we got for takeout while we’re all sitting at the park waiting for practice to start. I can still find those same benefits, which are that I’m asking about their day. I’m showing interest in them. We are connecting around the food.”

We have not completely eliminated UPF. But for the most part, I try to cook, choose whole food items and purchase better-quality ingredients. Over five years and thousands of decisions, our palates have shifted to prefer non-UPF foods. Cooking and baking have become routine. When you reach the stage where UPF is no longer desirable taste-wise, and regular cooking is as habitual as brushing your teeth, opting out of UPF becomes less daunting.

Without fail, every expert I spoke with emphasized the structural factors that make it difficult for consumers to reduce UPF consumption. Their prevalence, coupled with time and cost pressures mean that UPF often makes the most sense. There will be unexpected work emergencies or illnesses that make cooking from scratch impossible. Some days are full of extracurriculars and social events, and you won’t have time to bake or cook. You may attend catered events, meetings or functions where you have no control over the food.

The vast majority of us will eat at least some UPF. Your children will eat UPF – especially at birthday parties and play dates. But that’s not failure, just reality. Being realistic about your options can help you stay consistent instead of giving up entirely, and that’s ultimately what got me and my family to this point. When it comes to UPF consumption, don’t let perfect become the enemy of good.

Learn techniques: “Once you know how to saute, it doesn’t matter what you put in the pan.”
Develop knife skills: “If people want to get more comfortable in the kitchen, they should take a knife skills class. Those skills are a massive upscale for confidence in the kitchen.”

Know your taste: “Know what you like, then create that, because that’s the starting place. Don’t start by making miso brownies when the core is to make a really good brownie that you like, because then you’ll do it again.”

Wolfson found in her research that “when people cook more at home, they consume fewer ultra-processed foods.” She notes that even when people are cooking frequently, the majority of their calories often still come from UPF. Try not to feel defeated if you notice something similar; this speaks to the ubiquity of UPF, she says: “If you picture a typical American grocery store, almost 70% of the foods in the store are ultra processed … that’s what’s mostly available to us.”

Stankiewicz says to approach baking with fearlessness. “Messing up is part of it and is really how you learn,” she says, telling me that brownies were created by accident when a baker didn’t add enough flour to the cake.

The next step is to find trusted recipe sources. Start with recipes that are not overly intimidating or complicated. Read the recipe through before beginning, then gather the ingredients. Organize the ingredients (mise-en-place) – and then just do it!
Consuming UPF is easy. It takes very little time and mental capacity to, say, unwrap and heat something. Try to make cooking and baking at home similarly easy.

First, master a few dishes. “Find a handful of recipes or techniques that are accessible in terms of ingredients, cost, number of ingredients, and how long they’re going to take to make. Then just get really good at those,” says Stankiewicz.
Second, build a pantry. Keep a stock of frequently used ingredients. My husband and I maintain a shared shopping list, and if we notice something running low, we update the list.
Third, make more than you need. Leftovers are great. Even better are meals that can be frozen and reheated because your past self did all the work.

We have a rule at the table: no phones or toys during mealtimes. We have conversations instead (often mundane and not terribly deep). Our meals aren’t long; they usually take 20 minutes from when we sit down to when my youngest is done.

“The research is really clear that family meals, where people are eating together, have a huge positive impact on child development across many different areas in terms of school performance, mental health and social engagement,” says Rappaport.

It can be difficult when schedules are varied. Rappaport has older children and strives to fit in three dinners together each week around work and extracurriculars. It’s less about “having a Norman Rockwell meal at the dinner table” than finding opportunities to engage, she says. “It can be burritos that we got for takeout while we’re all sitting at the park waiting for practice to start. I can still find those same benefits, which are that I’m asking about their day. I’m showing interest in them. We are connecting around the food.”

We have not completely eliminated UPF. But for the most part, I try to cook, choose whole food items and purchase better-quality ingredients. Over five years and thousands of decisions, our palates have shifted to prefer non-UPF foods. Cooking and baking have become routine. When you reach the stage where UPF is no longer desirable taste-wise, and regular cooking is as habitual as brushing your teeth, opting out of UPF becomes less daunting.

Without fail, every expert I spoke with emphasized the structural factors that make it difficult for consumers to reduce UPF consumption. Their prevalence, coupled with time and cost pressures mean that UPF often makes the most sense. There will be unexpected work emergencies or illnesses that make cooking from scratch impossible. Some days are full of extracurriculars and social events, and you won’t have time to bake or cook. You may attend catered events, meetings or functions where you have no control over the food.

The vast majority of us will eat at least some UPF. Your children will eat UPF – especially at birthday parties and play dates. But that’s not failure, just reality. Being realistic about your options can help you stay consistent instead of giving up entirely, and that’s ultimately what got me and my family to this point. When it comes to UPF consumption, don’t let perfect become the enemy of good.

articles.title: At greyhound life drawing, artists try to tackle complicated shapes
articles.summary: With soulful eyes and ‘reverse chicken legs’, greyhounds are challenging to sketch. At a fundraising event in Melbourne, Josh Nicholas tries his hand I’ve done life drawing before, but this time I am a bit worried. The woman across the room from me has done life drawing too, “but not with dogs”. The problem with drawing people from life is they tend to move. The same is true of greyhounds, but they also don’t understand when you ask them to hold a pose. Continue reading...
articles.content:
With soulful eyes and ‘reverse chicken legs’, greyhounds are challenging to sketch. At a fundraising event in Melbourne, Josh Nicholas tries his hand

I’ve done life drawing before, but this time I am a bit worried. The woman across the room from me has done life drawing too, “but not with dogs”.

The problem with drawing people from life is they tend to move. The same is true of greyhounds, but they also don’t understand when you ask them to hold a pose.

As I enter TKO Tattoos, where greyhound life drawing classes are held once a month or so, I’m greeted by a white whippet wearing a fetching yellow, blue and black striped jumper. “That’s Fettuccine,” says TK, the parlour’s owner.

We’re in Eltham, about 20km north of Melbourne’s centre, in a small industrial building set back from the road. The walls are lined with tattoo designs and other art, many of them stylised greyhounds. “I’ve always wanted my own little art studio,” TK says.

Finding I have no treats, Fettuccine loses interest and wanders towards the back of the studio to join the other star of the show. Nina, a smallish black greyhound, is lounging on a plush red cushion. She’s wearing a floral yellow collar and a jumper that matches Fettuccine’s – even inside, the weather is brisk.

Whippets and greyhounds look similar, with long, narrow heads and thin, muscular bodies. Whippets tend to be smaller than greyhounds. And, unlike greyhounds, they aren’t raced commercially in Australia, only by hobbyists.

TK’s mum rescued greyhounds, so they’ve lived with these dogs since childhood. Now they host classes to raise money for Greyt Greys , the rescue service through which they adopted Nina.

Victoria has one of the largest greyhound racing industries in the world, with thousands of dogs needing rehabilitation and good homes each year. This life drawing fundraiser sold out in about two days.

I take one of the seats oriented around Nina’s cushion as about 20 other artists file in – Fettuccine greeting every single person at the door.

There’s a wide mix, including some who have never taken a life drawing class. But there’s plenty of greyhound lovers.

“There are six greyhound ‘faces’,” says Charlotte, sitting next to me. She has owned four greyhounds over the years; her current two dogs “have the same face”.

It’s my first of many lessons about greyhounds over the next two hours – such as did you know some greyhounds have translucent legs? It’s a skin flap on the back, really. I try hard but can’t see it. Maybe it’s just the way Nina is sitting.

I do some warm-up sketches of Fettuccine as he does the rounds, diligently investigating our bags for any undeclared treats. I keep drawing his face too narrow and mucking up the angle and curve of his hind legs.

Someone hisses: “They’re just goofy-shaped animals!” That’s it! Have you ever noticed how weirdly shaped whippets are? Someone else mentions a “reverse chicken leg”. This may not be correct anatomically, but just about nails the problem.

I switch over to Nina, who is facing me with her front paws off the end of the cushion. She has gorgeous, deep-brown eyes that are hard not to stare into. She’s five years old, says TK’s partner Leah, who has Nina’s leash clipped on to her belt. Nina “was intended to race”, but they don’t know whether she made it on to a track, she says.

By my second attempt, Nina has shifted to face away from me. I draw the long sweep of her belly, all the way around her leg. Mostly from memory, as she keeps moving. Do greyhounds’ knees always point away from them when they lay down? Are those even knees? This leads to a heated group discussion.

“I keep drawing and thinking: this can’t be right! But no, that’s how she’s actually sitting,” says Emma, Charlotte’s partner. “I’ve just been ignoring the paws.”

So have I – they’re a very complicated shape with lots of joints. TK saves us with cards they’ve drawn of greyhound body parts, showing how to use circles and other shapes to construct the joints. It definitely helps.

If you’ve ever learned to draw a human head via the Loomis method , or break down the human body into a series of stacked boxes , then you’ve seen this approach. The Loomis method starts a drawing of a head with a circle and uses that to work out the facial features. Similarly, you can think of a greyhound’s head as a circle with a cone attached. The joints in their lower legs and paws are a series of circles you slowly flesh out into a cylinder.

By my sixth sketch we’re winding down. We leave our drawings on our chairs and take a tour round the room. There’s a huge variety of sketches, including some incredible, quick renderings in pencil and charcoal.

In the end, I’m not overjoyed with any of my drawings. But it’s not because of Nina and Fettuccine’s reverse chicken legs, or that they wouldn’t sit still. I just need practice. Perhaps a lot of it. And I’ll remember to bring treats for Fettuccine next time.

TKO Tattoos’ next greyhound life drawing fundraiser is on 8 August at 10.30am at the Round Shader Club in Eltham, Melbourne

articles.title: Seth Meyers on Trump calling Canadian wildfire cost incalculable: ‘So he didn’t write that post’
articles.summary: Late-night host spoke about president’s recent social media posts, including comments on the World Cup and Canada Seth Meyers discussed Donald Trump ’s most recent comments on the World Cup, including his claim that players don’t work hard enough. On Late Night, the host spoke about a social media post from the US president where he thanked Rudy Giuliani’s son, Andrew, for helping to organise the tournament in the US. Continue reading...
articles.content:
Late-night host spoke about president’s recent social media posts, including comments on the World Cup and Canada

Seth Meyers discussed Donald Trump ’s most recent comments on the World Cup, including his claim that players don’t work hard enough.

On Late Night, the host spoke about a social media post from the US president where he thanked Rudy Giuliani’s son, Andrew, for helping to organise the tournament in the US.

“It was already the biggest sporting event in the world,” Meyers pointed out. “It’s not like he sold 7m pickleball tickets.”

This week has also seen Trump engaging in a photo opportunity with the most recent Little League softball champions in the White House. “Wow, still chasing that World Cup final high, huh?” Meyers said.

After wildfires led to air quality issues over the past week in parts of the US, Trump criticised Canada for failing to take preventive measures. In a social media post, he said the “cost is incalculable”.

Trump has also said that he wishes he could switch places with Cristiano Ronaldo, as he believes that his life is much easier than his. He said he would like to switch for about a month or so.

He added: “Watching Ronaldo run full-speed up and down a soccer pitch and you’re like, ‘That guy doesn’t work as hard as President Trump.’”

This week has also seen the American Heart Association claim that five cups of coffee is a safe amount for someone to have in one day. Meyers showed a picture of Trump sleeping and joked “but you know, maybe start with one”.

A study also showed that 34% of people who choose to eat in their car tend to pick easier and more convenient food options. “That makes sense, it’s probably not the time or the place for tapas,” he said.

A German man has also broken a Guinness World Record this week after popping 60 balloons in 30 seconds using a plunger. “So the toilet is still clogged?” Meyers joked that his wife then said.

Trump has also claimed that he is “No 1” on TikTok. “‘Guess again, bitch,’ said Jimothy,” Meyers said referencing the Seattle raccoon who has recently gone viral.

“So glad to see it finally happening for Jimothy, he’s been at it for a long time,” he added.
